interpretive_questions: Does the promise 'upon the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee' limit full application to those in the Land of Israel, or is this a general principle with location-specific blessing enhancement? The moral principle appears universal while the specific promised benefit may be land-dependent.
notes: Fifth commandment: honor father and mother. Deuteronomy adds 'as the LORD thy God hath commanded thee.'

verse_text: Thou shalt not kill.
law_summary: Prohibition against killing/murder
addressee_in_text: all Israel (second person singular addressing the nation)
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 5:17 (repetition in second giving of the law); Genesis 9:6 (principle given to Noah); Numbers 35:16-21 (elaboration on murder vs manslaughter)
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental moral commandment given as part of the Ten Commandments with no temporal limitations, prerequisites, or conditional language. The prohibition against murder is presented as an absolute moral principle applicable to all members of the covenant community.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:13 states the prohibition directly without qualifiers. Deuteronomy 5:17 repeats it identically. Genesis 9:6 establishes the principle pre-Israel, and Numbers 35 elaborates on implementation, suggesting ongoing relevance.
interpretive_questions: While the Hebrew 'ratsach' specifically refers to unlawful killing (murder) rather than all killing (such as in warfare or capital punishment), the basic moral principle appears universal and ongoing based on its inclusion in the fundamental covenant law and lack of limiting language.
notes: Sixth commandment: prohibition against murder.

verse_text: Thou shalt not commit adultery.
law_summary: Prohibition against committing adultery
addressee_in_text: all Israel (second person singular addressing the nation)
classification_reasoning: This is one of the Ten Commandments given as foundational moral law to Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language in this verse, it is presented as fundamental moral statute with no conditions, time limits, or prerequisites. The commandment requires no temple, priesthood, or land to observe.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:14 states the prohibition directly with no qualifying conditions. Leviticus 20:10 provides the penalty, indicating this is ongoing law. Deuteronomy 5:18 repeats it in Moses' restatement of the law.
interpretive_questions: While this specific verse lacks explicit 'forever' language, it appears within the Ten Commandments which are presented as foundational law. The question is whether the context of the Decalogue itself implies perpetuity even without explicit eternal language in each individual commandment.
notes: Seventh commandment: prohibition against adultery.

verse_text: Thou shalt not steal.
law_summary: Prohibition against stealing or taking another person's property
classification_reasoning: This is one of the Ten Commandments given as fundamental moral law to Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it is presented as a foundational moral principle requiring no special conditions, locations, or roles to observe. The commandment is repeated multiple times in Torah, indicating its fundamental and ongoing nature.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:15 within the context of the Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:1-17); repetition in Leviticus 19:11 'Ye shall not steal' and Deuteronomy 5:19 identical to Exodus 20:15
interpretive_questions: While clearly a fundamental moral law, the text itself does not contain explicit perpetual language like 'forever' or 'throughout your generations.' The ongoing nature is inferred from its placement in the Ten Commandments and repeated emphasis, but this represents interpretive reasoning rather than explicit textual statement of duration.
notes: Eighth commandment: prohibition against stealing.

verse_text: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour.
law_summary: Prohibition against giving false testimony against one's neighbor
addressee_in_text: all Israel (second person singular addressing the people)
other_torah_refs: Exodus 23:1 ('Thou shalt not raise a false report'), Deuteronomy 5:20 (repeat of Ten Commandments), Deuteronomy 19:16-21 (detailed laws about false witnesses)
classification_reasoning: This is one of the Ten Commandments given as fundamental moral law to Israel. While it lacks explicit 'forever' language, it is presented as foundational covenant law with no contextual limitations, requires no special prerequisites, and is reinforced throughout Torah with detailed applications.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:16 within the context of the Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:1-17); reinforced in Exodus 23:1 and Deuteronomy 5:20; elaborated with procedural details in Deuteronomy 19:16-21
interpretive_questions: While the Ten Commandments are clearly presented as fundamental covenant law, the specific eternal duration must be inferred from their foundational nature and repeated emphasis rather than explicit perpetual language in this particular commandment.
notes: Ninth commandment: prohibition against false witness. Deuteronomy uses 'vain' (shav) instead of 'false' (sheker).

verse_text:
Thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s house, thou shalt not covet thy neighbour’s wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, nor his ox, nor his ass, nor any thing that
is thy neighbour’s.
law_summary: Do not covet your neighbor's possessions including house, wife, servants, animals, or anything belonging to your neighbor
addressee_in_text: all Israel (second person singular addressing the nation)
classification_reasoning: This is part of the Ten Commandments given as fundamental moral law to Israel. While lacking explicit perpetual language in this verse, it functions as an ongoing statute governing internal attitudes and desires that require no external prerequisites to observe.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:17 - direct prohibition against coveting; Deuteronomy 5:21 - identical restatement showing ongoing importance; Context within the Ten Commandments (Exodus 20:1-17) given as fundamental covenant stipulations
interpretive_questions: Does the lack of explicit 'forever' language in the Ten Commandments indicate different duration than laws with explicit perpetual language, or should the foundational nature of these commandments imply permanence? The Torah presents these as fundamental covenant stipulations but without the temporal modifiers found in ritual laws.
notes: Tenth commandment: prohibition against coveting. Deuteronomy separates 'covet' and 'desire' into distinct prohibitions and lists wife before house.

verse_text: Ye shall not make with me gods of silver, neither shall ye make unto you gods of gold.
law_summary: Prohibition against making gods of silver or gold
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental prohibition against idolatry given as part of the Ten Commandments. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it appears in the context of foundational covenant law and is reinforced throughout Torah. The prohibition requires no special conditions, location, or role to observe.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:23 - direct prohibition; Deuteronomy 7:25 - 'The graven images of their gods shall ye burn with fire: thou shalt not desire the silver or gold that is on them'; Exodus 32:31 - Moses refers to Israel making 'gods of gold'
interpretive_questions: Does the specific mention of 'silver' and 'gold' limit this to only precious metal idols, or is this representative of all idol-making? The broader context of the second commandment (Exodus 20:4-6) suggests this may be a specific application of the general idol prohibition.
notes: Prohibition against making gods of silver or gold. Related to the second commandment but specifies the materials.

verse_text: An altar of earth thou shalt make unto me, and shalt sacrifice thereon thy burnt offerings, and thy peace offerings, thy sheep, and thine oxen: in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee.
law_summary: Make an altar of earth for burnt offerings and peace offerings of sheep and oxen in places where God records His name
other_torah_refs: Exodus 20:25-26 (additional altar specifications), Deuteronomy 27:5-7 (stone altar instructions)
classification_reasoning: Command lacks explicit perpetual language but is given as ongoing instruction. The phrase 'in all places where I record my name' suggests flexibility of location but requires divine designation of appropriate places.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:24 - 'in all places where I record my name I will come unto thee, and I will bless thee' indicates ongoing divine blessing contingent on proper implementation
interpretive_questions: Does 'in all places where I record my name' require specific divine revelation for each location? Is priestly involvement implied for burnt/peace offerings even though not explicitly stated? Can this be fulfilled without formal priesthood or does it assume Levitical system? What constitutes God 'recording His name' in a place?
categories: LOVE_GOD > WORSHIP_AND_OFFERINGS > The Altar and Place of Worship
notes: Instruction to build an earthen altar for offerings at places where God records His name.

verse_text: And if thou wilt make me an altar of stone, thou shalt not build it of hewn stone: for if thou lift up thy tool upon it, thou hast polluted it.
law_summary: If making a stone altar, use unhewn stones without tools to avoid polluting it
classification_reasoning: This is a conditional law ('if thou wilt make') with no temporal limitations. The condition is building a stone altar, and the requirement is to use unhewn stones. No temple, priesthood, or specific location is required - just the conditional act of altar construction.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:25 'And if thou wilt make me an altar of stone' - conditional language. Deuteronomy 27:5-6 repeats the same requirement for the altar on Mount Ebal, confirming this as an ongoing statute rather than a one-time instruction.
interpretive_questions: Does this apply only to altars for sacrifice, or any stone structure dedicated to God? The text doesn't specify the purpose of the altar, only its construction method. Also unclear whether this principle extends beyond literal stone altars to other forms of worship construction.
categories: LOVE_GOD > WORSHIP_AND_OFFERINGS > The Altar and Place of Worship
notes: Stone altars must use unhewn stones; no tools may be used on them.

verse_text: Neither shalt thou go up by steps unto mine altar, that thy nakedness be not discovered thereon.
law_summary: Do not use steps to go up to the altar to avoid exposing nakedness
requires_specific_role: those who serve at the altar
other_torah_refs: Exodus 28:42-43 (priestly garments to cover nakedness), Leviticus 6:10-11 (priestly garments for altar service)
classification_reasoning: This is a statute about proper altar construction and service protocol. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it is given as part of the broader legal code without temporal limitations. However, it absolutely requires both an altar and those who serve at it.
textual_evidence: Exodus 20:26 - the command is given as a direct prohibition without temporal qualifiers, indicating ongoing applicability when the prerequisite conditions (altar/service) exist
interpretive_questions: Does this apply only to the specific altar design mentioned in context, or to any altar used for worship? The broader context discusses altar construction generally, suggesting broader application beyond just the temple altar.
categories: LOVE_GOD > WORSHIP_AND_OFFERINGS > The Altar and Place of Worship
notes: Prohibition against using steps to ascend the altar, to prevent exposure of nakedness. Related to priestly modesty requirements.

verse_text: If thou buy an Hebrew servant, six years he shall serve: and in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing.
law_summary: Hebrew servants must be freed after six years of service without payment
addressee_in_text: Israel (implied by broader context of Exodus civil laws)
classification_reasoning: While the law lacks explicit perpetual language, it appears within the broader civil code for Israel. The practical application requires a Hebrew society with Hebrew servants operating under Torah law, which currently doesn't exist in a complete form.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:2 establishes the principle; Deuteronomy 15:12 states 'If thy brother, an Hebrew man, or an Hebrew woman, be sold unto thee' indicating ongoing statute for Hebrew society
interpretive_questions: Does this law apply only within the land of Israel or wherever Hebrews live under Torah law? What constitutes 'Hebrew servant' in modern context - only Israelites or broader Hebrew community? Can this law function properly without a complete Torah-based legal system in place?
notes: Hebrew servants must be freed in the seventh year. Foundation of Hebrew servant law.

verse_text: If he came in by himself, he shall go out by himself: if he were married, then his wife shall go out with him.
law_summary: A Hebrew slave who enters service alone shall leave alone, but if married when entering, his wife shall leave with him
addressee_in_text: Israel (implied from broader context of Exodus 21)
classification_reasoning: This is part of the civil law code governing Hebrew slavery. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it is presented as ongoing legal statute within the broader mishpatim (judgments) section. However, it requires the institution of Hebrew slavery to be operative.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:3 is part of the civil ordinances beginning in Exodus 21:1 'Now these are the judgments which thou shalt set before them.' The law assumes the ongoing practice of Hebrew servitude as regulated by Torah.
interpretive_questions: Does this law apply only when Hebrew slavery is practiced according to Torah regulations, or does it establish principles for any analogous modern employment/service relationships? The prerequisite of having a functioning Hebrew slavery system as defined by Torah may not currently exist.
notes: Marital status determines whether the servant leaves alone or with a wife upon release.

verse_text: If his master have given him a wife, and she have born him sons or daughters; the wife and her children shall be her master’s, and he shall go out by himself.
law_summary: When a master gives a Hebrew slave a wife and they have children, the wife and children remain the master's property when the slave's term ends
requires_specific_role: Hebrew slaves and masters within Israelite society
other_torah_refs: Exodus 21:2-6 (broader Hebrew slave laws), Deuteronomy 15:12-18 (parallel slave release laws)
classification_reasoning: This law is part of the civil ordinances governing Hebrew slavery within Israelite society. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it functions as an ongoing statute for regulating master-slave relationships. However, the entire Hebrew slavery system described here appears to be contextually completed as it required functioning Israelite civil courts and the specific socio-economic structure of ancient Israel.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:1 introduces these as 'judgments which thou shalt set before them' - indicating ongoing civil statutes. The law itself (21:4) provides procedural guidance without temporal limitations but within the broader context of Hebrew slavery laws.
interpretive_questions: Does this law apply in any context where Hebrew slavery might be practiced today, or was it specifically designed for the ancient Israelite socio-economic system? The Torah provides the procedure but doesn't explicitly address whether Hebrew slavery itself is an eternal institution or a contextual provision for ancient Israel.
notes: If the master provided the servant with a wife, the wife and children remain with the master at release.

verse_text: And if the servant shall plainly say, I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free:
law_summary: A Hebrew servant may choose to remain with his master permanently if he declares love for his master, wife, and children
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 15:16-17 (similar provision with additional details about the ear-piercing ceremony)
classification_reasoning: This is a conditional law that applies when the specific circumstance exists (Hebrew servant choosing permanent service). No explicit perpetual language, but part of the broader civil code given to Israel. The law itself doesn't contain time limitations.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:5 states the condition ('if the servant shall plainly say'), and Deuteronomy 15:16-17 repeats the provision with procedural details, indicating this is an ongoing statute rather than a one-time instruction.
interpretive_questions: Does this law apply only within the Hebrew servitude system as outlined in Torah, or could it apply in any master-servant relationship? The law is conditional on the existence of Hebrew servitude practices, which may not currently exist in the prescribed Torah form. Also unclear whether this requires a functioning Israelite civil society to implement properly.
notes: Provision for a servant who voluntarily chooses to remain permanently.

verse_text: Then his master shall bring him unto the judges; he shall also bring him to the door, or unto the door post; and his master shall bore his ear through with an aul; and he shall serve him for ever.
law_summary: Procedure for making a Hebrew slave permanent when he chooses to remain with his master
forever_phrase: and he shall serve him for ever
requires_specific_role: judges, master, Hebrew slave who chooses to remain
addressee_in_text: Israel (implied from broader context of civil laws)
classification_reasoning: Contains explicit 'for ever' language regarding the service relationship. However, requires functioning Hebrew slavery system, Jewish civil courts ('judges'), and likely Jewish sovereign territory for full implementation.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:6 'and he shall serve him for ever' - direct eternal language. Deuteronomy 15:16-17 repeats the procedure with similar permanence language.
interpretive_questions: Does 'for ever' apply to the individual servant's lifetime only, or does it extend to descendants? What constitutes the proper 'judges' referenced in the text? Can this law function in diaspora communities or does it require sovereign Jewish jurisdiction?
notes: Procedure for ear-boring of a servant who chooses permanent service: brought to judges, ear pierced at the doorpost.

verse_text: And if a man sell his daughter to be a maidservant, she shall not go out as the menservants do.
law_summary: A female Hebrew servant sold by her father cannot be released in the same manner as male servants
addressee_in_text: general address to Israel (implied from broader context)
classification_reasoning: Part of civil law code in Exodus 21-23 with no temporal limitations stated. The law establishes different procedures for female servants versus male servants, with no conditions requiring temple, priesthood, or specific land. As a civil statute regulating Hebrew servitude, it appears to be ongoing law.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:7 states the law directly without temporal qualifiers. The broader context of Exodus 21:1 introduces these as 'judgments' (mishpatim) to be set before Israel, suggesting ongoing civil statutes.
interpretive_questions: Does this law require the broader Hebrew servitude system to be active to be applicable? The modern absence of Hebrew servitude practices raises questions about practical implementation, though the legal principle itself contains no textual expiration.
notes: Female Hebrew servants have different release terms than male servants.

verse_text: If she please not her master, who hath betrothed her to himself, then shall he let her be redeemed: to sell her unto a strange nation he shall have no power, seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her.
law_summary: If a master who has betrothed a Hebrew maidservant to himself is displeased with her, he must allow her to be redeemed and cannot sell her to foreigners
addressee_in_text: Israelites (context of civil laws given to Moses)
classification_reasoning: This is part of the civil law code regarding Hebrew servitude. While containing no explicit perpetual language, it establishes ongoing legal principles for Hebrew servant relationships. However, the specific context of Hebrew maidservant betrothal practices described here are no longer operationally relevant in current contexts.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:8 - conditional law regarding Hebrew maidservant betrothal and redemption rights; part of broader civil law section in Exodus 21-23
interpretive_questions: Does the underlying principle of protecting vulnerable persons from exploitation in covenant relationships carry forward even if the specific Hebrew servitude context no longer applies? Is this law culturally bound to ancient Near Eastern marriage/servitude customs or does it establish enduring justice principles?
notes: If a master betroths a female servant and is displeased, she must be allowed to be redeemed; he cannot sell her to foreigners.

verse_text: And if he have betrothed her unto his son, he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters.
law_summary: If a master betroths his female servant to his son, he must treat her with the rights and status of a daughter
requires_specific_role: master who has betrothed his female servant to his son
classification_reasoning: This is a case law within the mishpatim (judgments) addressing a specific scenario in ancient servant relationships. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it represents an ethical principle about honoring commitments and treating people with dignity. However, the specific legal framework of Hebrew servanthood that this addresses is no longer practiced.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:9 - part of the broader legal code in Exodus 21-23 that addresses various civil and social situations. The law is conditional ('if he have betrothed her') and addresses a specific relationship dynamic within the servant system.
interpretive_questions: Does this represent only a specific case law for ancient servant relationships, or does it establish an eternal principle about honoring commitments made regarding marriages? The underlying principle of treating betrothed individuals with proper dignity may transcend the specific servant context, but the exact legal mechanism described requires the Hebrew servant system to be operational.
notes: A female servant betrothed to the master's son must be treated as a daughter.

verse_text:
If he take him another
wife; her food, her raiment, and her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish.
law_summary: A man who takes a second wife must not reduce the food, clothing, or marital rights of his first wife
classification_reasoning: This is presented as a straightforward legal statute within the civil laws section (Mishpatim) without temporal limitations. The law addresses ongoing marital obligations and requires no special prerequisites beyond the marital context itself.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:10 uses direct imperative language 'shall he not diminish' without any qualifying temporal or conditional language, indicating an ongoing legal standard
interpretive_questions: The broader context of polygamy laws and whether this applies only when polygamy occurs or establishes a general principle of marital obligation maintenance. Also unclear if this is specifically about taking a second wife or includes other scenarios of potential neglect.
notes: If a man takes another wife, the first wife's food, clothing, and conjugal rights must not be diminished. This applies to the servant-wife context but establishes a general principle of marital obligations.

verse_text: And if he do not these three unto her, then shall she go out free without money.
law_summary: If a master fails to provide food, clothing, and marital rights to a female Hebrew slave he has married, she shall go free without payment
classification_reasoning: This is part of the civil legal code with no explicit temporal limitation, but its application depends on the existence of Hebrew slavery system which Torah regulates but doesn't currently exist
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:11 provides conditional protection within the broader Mishpatim (civil laws) section beginning at Exodus 21:1
interpretive_questions: Does this law apply only when Hebrew slavery system exists as regulated by Torah, or does it establish a permanent principle of protection for vulnerable women in servitude relationships? The law assumes the framework of Hebrew slavery regulations outlined in the same chapter.
notes: If the three obligations (food, clothing, marital rights) are not met, the female servant goes free without payment.

verse_text:
And if a man lie not in wait, but God deliver
him into his hand; then I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee.
law_summary: God will provide cities of refuge for those who kill accidentally without premeditation
other_torah_refs: Numbers 35:9-34, Deuteronomy 4:41-43, Deuteronomy 19:1-13, Joshua 20:1-9, Numbers 35:11, Numbers 35:25, Deuteronomy 4:42, Deuteronomy 19:2, Deuteronomy 19:4, Deuteronomy 19:5, Deuteronomy 19:7
classification_reasoning: While this verse lacks explicit perpetual language, the cities of refuge system is developed extensively throughout Torah as an ongoing judicial requirement. The law requires possession of the land of Israel to establish the designated cities, making it prerequisite pending rather than currently applicable in exile.
textual_evidence: Numbers 35:29 'And these things shall be for a statute of judgment unto you throughout your generations in all your dwellings.' Deuteronomy 19:9 shows the system was designed to expand as territory expanded, indicating ongoing nature.
interpretive_questions: Does the principle of refuge for accidental killers apply even without the specific cities? Can modern legal systems fulfill this Torah requirement, or must it be the exact six cities in Israel? The generational language appears in Numbers 35:29 but not in this specific verse - how does that affect interpretation of this particular reference?
notes: Establishes the principle of cities of refuge for unintentional killing. Elaborated extensively in Numbers and Deuteronomy.

verse_text: And he that smiteth his father, or his mother, shall be surely put to death.
law_summary: Anyone who strikes their father or mother must be put to death
other_torah_refs: Exodus 21:17 (cursing parents), Leviticus 20:9 (cursing parents), Deuteronomy 21:18-21 (rebellious son), Deuteronomy 27:16 (dishonoring parents)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil/criminal law within Israel's legal code without explicit perpetual language, but part of the broader moral framework. Requires judicial system and authority to execute capital punishment.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:15 states the law directly within the mishpatim (judgments) section. The repeated emphasis on honoring parents throughout Torah (Exodus 20:12, Deuteronomy 5:16) and severe penalties for dishonoring them suggests this reflects enduring moral principle.
interpretive_questions: Does this require the formal Israelite judicial system and Sanhedrin authority? Is this limited to the covenant community of Israel or universal? What constitutes 'striking' versus other forms of violence? How does this relate to the broader principle of honoring parents versus the specific penalty mechanism?

verse_text: And he that stealeth a man, and selleth him, or if he be found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death.
law_summary: Anyone who kidnaps a person and sells them into slavery, or is found in possession of a kidnapped person, shall be put to death
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 24:7 - 'If a man be found stealing any of his brethren of the children of Israel, and maketh merchandise of him, or selleth him; then that thief shall die'
classification_reasoning: This is a civil/criminal statute within the mishpatim (judgments) given to Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it's part of the ongoing legal framework for Israel. The death penalty aspect requires judicial authority and organized community structure.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:16 states the law directly without temporal limitations. Deuteronomy 24:7 repeats the same principle with slight variation, indicating its importance as ongoing statute. Located within the civil laws given after the Ten Commandments.
interpretive_questions: Does execution of capital punishment require the formal Israelite judicial system, priesthood involvement for legal determinations, or can it be applied by any constituted authority? The text doesn't specify the mechanism for carrying out the death penalty or required judicial procedures.

verse_text: And he that curseth his father, or his mother, shall surely be put to death.
law_summary: Anyone who curses their father or mother must be executed

Does 'requires_land_israel' mean specifically the land or any constituted Israelite community with proper authority? What constitutes 'cursing' versus other forms of dishonor covered in the fifth commandment?

verse_text:
And if men strive together, and one smite another with a stone, or with
his fist, and he die not, but keepeth
his bed:

No explicit duration language is given, but the nature of the law as foundational civil legislation implies ongoing application.

Question remains whether civil laws require an Israelite judicial system to be properly implemented, though the law itself doesn't specify this requirement.

verse_text:
If he rise again, and walk abroad upon his staff, then shall he that smote
him be quit: only he shall pay
for the loss of his time, and shall cause
him to be thoroughly healed.

verse_text: And if a man smite his servant, or his maid, with a rod, and he die under his hand; he shall be surely punished.
law_summary: If a master strikes his servant with a rod and the servant dies, the master must be punished
classification_reasoning: This is a civil justice statute within the broader legal code given to Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it is presented as part of the ongoing legal framework (mishpatim) that follows the Ten Commandments. The conditional structure ('if a man') indicates a standing law for whenever the situation arises, not a one-time instruction.

interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'surely punished' - death penalty or other punishment? Does this apply only within Israelite society or to all who follow Torah? How does this relate to modern employment relationships versus ancient servant/master relationships?
notes: Punishment for a master who kills a servant with a rod. Intersects both bodily harm law and servant protection law.

verse_text:
Notwithstanding, if he continue a day or two, he shall not be punished: for he
is his money.
law_summary: If a slave survives a day or two after being struck by his master, the master shall not be punished because the slave is his property

interpretive_questions: Does this law apply only within the specific Hebrew slavery system outlined in Exodus 21:2-11, or to any master-slave relationship? The law assumes the existence of slavery but doesn't explicitly limit its temporal scope. Also unclear whether this applies only when Israel has judicial authority to enforce civil laws.

verse_text:
If men strive, and hurt a woman with child, so that her fruit depart
from her, and yet no mischief follow: he shall be surely punished, according as the woman’s husband will lay upon him; and he shall pay as the judges
determine.
law_summary: If men fighting injure a pregnant woman causing premature birth but no further harm, the responsible party must pay compensation as determined by the husband and judges
classification_reasoning: This is a civil tort law within the broader Mishpatim (judgments) section. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it establishes a principle of justice for harm caused to pregnant women that appears to be an ongoing legal statute rather than a temporary or contextual command.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:22 - positioned within the civil law section following the Ten Commandments, presented as ongoing legal statute without temporal limitations
interpretive_questions: The law requires 'judges' to determine compensation - unclear if this refers to specifically appointed Israelite judges or any legitimate judicial authority. Also unclear how this interfaces with modern legal systems where such matters may already be covered by existing tort law.
notes: Compensation for causing a miscarriage (or premature birth) when no further harm occurs. The husband and judges determine the amount.

verse_text:
And if
any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life,
law_summary: If serious harm results from an incident, the punishment must be proportional - life for life
classification_reasoning: This is part of the judicial ordinances (mishpatim) given to Israel without explicit temporal limitations. The principle of proportional justice appears multiple times in Torah, suggesting ongoing validity. However, implementation requires established judicial system with proper authority.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:1 introduces this section as 'judgments which thou shalt set before them' - ongoing legal statutes. Repetition in Leviticus 24:17-20 and Deuteronomy 19:21 reinforces the enduring nature of this principle.
interpretive_questions: Does this require the full Israelite judicial system in the land, or can it apply wherever Israelite communities establish proper judicial procedures? The conditional nature ('if any mischief follow') makes it situational but doesn't limit its temporal scope.
notes: Lex talionis: life for life. Establishes the principle of proportional justice.

verse_text: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,
law_summary: Punishment must be proportional to the injury inflicted (lex talionis)
classification_reasoning: This is a judicial statute given within the broader legal code for Israel. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it is repeated multiple times in Torah and represents a fundamental principle of proportional justice. However, it requires an established judicial system with authority to implement such punishments.
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:24 within the broader context of Exodus 21:1 'Now these are the judgments which you shall set before them'; repeated in Leviticus 24:19-20 'And if a man causes a disfigurement of his neighbor; as he has done, so shall it be done to him: Breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth'; and Deuteronomy 19:21 'And your eye shall not pity; but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.'
interpretive_questions: Does this principle apply only to physical injuries or extend to all forms of proportional justice? Can this be implemented through monetary compensation or only literal physical retribution? What level of judicial authority is required to implement this law - local elders, formal courts, or only a complete theocratic system?
notes: Lex talionis continued: eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot.

verse_text: Burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.
law_summary: Punishment must be proportionate to the injury - burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe
classification_reasoning: Part of judicial code given to Israel as ongoing legal statute, but requires established judicial system with authority to implement physical punishments
textual_evidence: Exodus 21:25 within broader mishpatim (judgments) section; repeated in Leviticus 24:19-20 'fracture for fracture, eye for eye, tooth for tooth' and Deuteronomy 19:21 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot'
interpretive_questions: Does this require literal physical implementation or does it establish proportionality principle? Can this be applied in diaspora communities with limited judicial authority, or only in a fully sovereign Israelite judicial system? What constitutes proper judicial authority to implement such punishments?
notes: Lex talionis continued: burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe.

verse_text: And if a man smite the eye of his servant, or the eye of his maid, that it perish; he shall let him go free for his eye’s sake.

verse_text: And if he smite out his manservant’s tooth, or his maidservant’s tooth; he shall let him go free for his tooth’s sake.

verse_text:

verse_text: But if the ox were wont to push with his horn in time past, and it hath been testified to his owner, and he hath not kept him in, but that he hath killed a man or a woman; the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death.

interpretive_questions: The absence of explicit perpetual language raises questions about duration. The death penalty aspect may require proper judicial authority and process. Does this require a functioning Israelite court system for capital punishment implementation?

verse_text: If there be laid on him a sum of money, then he shall give for the ransom of his life whatsoever is laid upon him.

verse_text: If the ox shall push a manservant or a maidservant; he shall give unto their master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned.

verse_text: If a man shall steal an ox, or a sheep, and kill it, or sell it; he shall restore five oxen for an ox, and four sheep for a sheep.

verse_text:

there shall be blood
shed
 for him;

verse_text: If a man shall deliver unto his neighbour money or stuff to keep, and it be stolen out of the man’s house; if the thief be found, let him pay double.

It requires no temple, priesthood, or specific location, dealing with universal principles of justice and property protection that can be applied wherever Israelites live.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:7 - presented as a straightforward conditional law ('If a man shall deliver...') within the broader legal code given to Israel, with no temporal limitations stated
interpretive_questions: While the law itself is clear and applicable, questions remain about enforcement mechanisms in diaspora settings and whether 'double restitution' principle applies when modern legal systems have different penalty structures
notes: Laws regarding entrusted property (money or goods) stolen from the custodian: thief pays double.

verse_text:
If the thief be not found, then the master of the house shall be brought unto the judges,
to see whether he have put his hand unto his neighbour’s goods.
law_summary: If a thief is not found, the house owner must be brought before judges to determine if he stole his neighbor's goods
classification_reasoning: This is a civil judicial procedure without explicit perpetual language, but given within the broader legal code for Israel's justice system. It requires established judges and a functioning judicial system.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:8 - 'shall be brought unto the judges' indicates requirement for established judicial authority
interpretive_questions: Does 'judges' refer specifically to Torah-appointed judges in Israel's theocratic system, or can this principle apply through any legitimate judicial authority? The text assumes an established judicial system but doesn't specify its exact nature or geographic boundaries.
notes: If the thief is not found, the custodian must appear before judges to determine whether he himself misappropriated the goods.

verse_text:
For all manner of trespass,
whether it be for ox, for ass, for sheep, for raiment,
or for any manner of lost thing, which
another challengeth to be his, the cause of both parties shall come before the judges;
and whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour.
law_summary: In disputes over property ownership, both parties must bring their case before judges, and whoever is found guilty must pay double compensation to the other party
addressee_in_text: Israel (implicit in broader legal code context)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil judicial procedure within Torah's legal code without explicit perpetual language, but part of ongoing legal statutes. Requires established judicial system with Torah-based judges.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:9 - 'the cause of both parties shall come before the judges; and whom the judges shall condemn, he shall pay double unto his neighbour'
interpretive_questions: What constitutes proper 'judges' for this law - must they be formally appointed Torah judges or can any qualified judicial authority suffice? Does this require a complete Torah-based legal system to be properly implemented?
notes: General rule for property disputes: both parties come before judges; the guilty party pays double. This is a court procedure that also relates to property law.

verse_text:
If a man deliver unto his neighbour an ass, or an ox, or a sheep, or any beast, to keep; and it die, or be hurt, or driven away, no man seeing
it:
law_summary: If someone entrusts livestock to a neighbor for safekeeping and the animal dies, is injured, or is stolen without witnesses, specific legal procedures apply
addressee_in_text: all Israel (civil law applicable to community members)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil statute governing bailment/custody relationships between individuals. It contains no temporal limitations, requires no special institutions, and addresses ongoing social interactions that exist in any agricultural or pastoral society.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:10 presents this as a straightforward legal statute within the broader civil code (Exodus 21-23), with no qualifying language suggesting temporary application or special prerequisites
interpretive_questions: The verse provided appears incomplete (cuts off mid-sentence). The full legal procedure and consequences would need to be examined from the complete passage. Also, while the principle appears universally applicable, the specific animals mentioned (ass, ox, sheep) reflect the agricultural context of ancient Israel - application to modern equivalents would need consideration.
notes: Sets up the scenario for custodial liability when entrusted livestock dies, is hurt, or is driven away without witnesses.

verse_text:
Then shall an oath of the LORD be between them both, that he hath not put his hand unto his neighbour’s goods; and the owner of it shall accept
thereof, and he shall not make
it good.
law_summary: When someone's property is lost while in another's care, an oath before the LORD determines liability - if the caretaker swears he didn't steal it, the owner must accept this and the caretaker owes no compensation
classification_reasoning: This is a civil judicial procedure within the broader legal code given to Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it appears as an ongoing statute for resolving disputes about property care. The procedure requires only the parties involved and witnesses - no temple, priesthood, or specific location.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:11 presents this as part of the mishpatim (judgments/ordinances) section that follows the Ten Commandments, indicating ongoing legal procedures rather than one-time events. The procedural nature ('Then shall an oath of the LORD be between them both') suggests a standard judicial process.
interpretive_questions: What constitutes a proper 'oath of the LORD' in practice? Does this procedure require formal witnesses or can it be done privately? The text doesn't specify the exact mechanism for administering such oaths, which could affect practical application.
notes: Oath before the LORD resolves custodial disputes when there are no witnesses. The owner must accept the oath.

verse_text: And if it be stolen from him, he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof.
law_summary: If an animal under a person's care is stolen, the caretaker must make restitution to the owner
addressee_in_text: Israel (in context of civil laws given through Moses)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil restitution law within the mishpatim (judgments) section. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it is presented as an ongoing legal principle for property disputes. It requires no temple infrastructure and addresses universal situations of property care and theft.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:12 - 'And if it be stolen from him, he shall make restitution unto the owner thereof.' Part of the civil code in Exodus 21-23 given as ongoing legal statutes.
interpretive_questions: Does the absence of explicit perpetual language in civil laws indicate they are less binding than ceremonial laws with such language? How do civil mishpatim relate to the broader eternal covenant framework when they lack specific duration markers?
notes: If the animal is stolen from the custodian's care, the custodian must make restitution.

verse_text:
If it be torn in pieces,
then let him bring it
for
 witness,
and he shall not make good that which was torn.
law_summary: If a borrowed animal is torn by wild beasts, the borrower must bring evidence and is not liable to compensate the owner
classification_reasoning: This is a civil law regarding borrowing and liability that appears within the broader legal code (mishpatim) given to Israel. It contains no temporal limitations or specific requirements that would prevent current application. The law addresses a universal situation (borrowed property damaged by wild animals) with a practical legal principle.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:13 provides the specific rule without temporal qualifiers. It appears within the civil laws section (Exodus 21-23) that were given as ongoing statutes for Israelite society.
interpretive_questions: While the law itself is clear, questions remain about: 1) What constitutes sufficient 'witness' or evidence of the tearing, 2) Whether this applies only to animals or extends to other borrowed property damaged by forces beyond the borrower's control, 3) The relationship between this law and modern legal systems regarding bailment and liability
notes: If the animal is killed by a wild beast, the custodian brings the remains as evidence and is not liable.

verse_text:
And if a man borrow
ought of his neighbour, and it be hurt, or die, the owner thereof
being not with it, he shall surely make
it good.
law_summary: If someone borrows an animal from a neighbor and it is injured or dies while the owner is not present, the borrower must make full compensation
classification_reasoning: This is a civil statute given as part of the mishpatim (judgments) without temporal limitations. It establishes a fundamental principle of responsibility for borrowed property that functions independently of temple, priesthood, or specific geography. The law is presented as an ongoing legal standard rather than a temporary ordinance.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:14 states the law directly without qualifying conditions or time limitations. It appears within the broader legal code of Exodus 21-23 which establishes civil and social statutes for Israel.
interpretive_questions: While this specific formulation doesn't appear elsewhere in Torah, the principle may be reflected in broader property and responsibility laws. Question remains whether 'neighbor' implies only fellow Israelites or extends to all interactions.
notes: Borrower's liability when the borrowed animal is injured or dies in the owner's absence.

verse_text:
But if the owner thereof
be with it, he shall not make
it good: if it
be an hired
thing, it came for his hire.
law_summary: If the owner is present when his animal is injured or dies while being borrowed, or if the animal was hired, no compensation is required
addressee_in_text: all Israel (within broader civil law context)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil liability law given as part of the comprehensive legal code for Israel. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it is presented as an ongoing statute governing property relationships. It requires no special institutions and addresses universal human situations involving borrowed or hired property.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:15 presents this as a straightforward legal principle within the broader civil code (Exodus 21-23). The conditional structure ('if...then') indicates a standing legal principle rather than a one-time instruction.
interpretive_questions: Does this specific law apply only within Israelite communities, or to all civil relationships involving Torah-observant individuals? The broader context of Exodus 21-23 civil laws raises questions about their scope of application in diaspora situations.
notes: If the owner is present or the animal was hired, no additional compensation is required.

verse_text: And if a man entice a maid that is not betrothed, and lie with her, he shall surely endow her to be his wife.
law_summary: A man who seduces an unbetrothed virgin must pay the bride-price and marry her
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 22:28-29 (similar case with additional details), Deuteronomy 22:29
classification_reasoning: This is a civil law given within the broader legal code without temporal limitations. It addresses ongoing social situations that can occur in any generation and requires no special institutions to implement.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:16 uses standard legal formulation 'if a man...' indicating a general statute. The parallel in Deuteronomy 22:28-29 adds that 'he may not put her away all his days,' suggesting permanent application.
interpretive_questions: The law assumes cultural contexts of bride-price and parental consent that may not directly translate to modern legal systems. Questions remain about whether this is purely restitutive or creates actual marriage obligations, and how it interfaces with consent issues.
notes: A man who seduces an unbetrothed virgin must pay the bride-price and marry her. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 addresses the related case of rape.

verse_text: If her father utterly refuse to give her unto him, he shall pay money according to the dowry of virgins.
law_summary: If a father refuses to give his daughter in marriage to a seducer, the man must still pay the bride price equivalent to that of virgins
addressee_in_text: Israel/the community (part of civil law code)
other_torah_refs: Exodus 22:16 (preceding verse about seducing a virgin), Deuteronomy 22:28-29 (similar but distinct case of rape)
classification_reasoning: This is part of the civil law code given to Israel without temporal limitations. It addresses ongoing situations involving sexual misconduct and family protection. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it functions as a standing legal principle within the broader mishpatim (judgments) section.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:17 appears within the civil law section (Exodus 21-23) containing various ongoing legal statutes for Israel's governance. The conditional nature ('If her father utterly refuse...') indicates a standing law for recurring situations.
interpretive_questions: Questions remain about application in modern contexts where civil marriage systems differ from ancient Near Eastern practices, and whether this requires a functioning Israelite court system to adjudicate and enforce financial penalties.
notes: If the father refuses the marriage, the seducer must still pay the bride-price.

verse_text: Thou shalt not suffer a witch to live.
law_summary: Witches must be put to death
classification_reasoning: This is a capital punishment law within Torah's judicial system. While witchcraft prohibition appears ongoing, execution requires proper judicial authority and processes established in the land
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:18 states the prohibition directly. Leviticus 20:27 specifies stoning for mediums/spiritists. Deuteronomy 18:10-11 lists various occult practices as abominations
interpretive_questions: Does this require formal Israelite judicial courts and witnesses as specified elsewhere in Torah? Is the prohibition universal while execution method/authority is conditional on having proper Torah-based government? What constitutes 'witch' versus other occult practitioners listed in Deuteronomy?
notes: Death penalty for witchcraft/sorcery.

verse_text: Whosoever lieth with a beast shall surely be put to death.
law_summary: Anyone who has sexual relations with an animal must be executed
classification_reasoning: This is a capital punishment law within Israel's civil/criminal code. While the moral prohibition appears ongoing, the specific penalty of execution requires a functioning civil authority structure to carry out death sentences according to Torah procedure.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:19 states the death penalty; Leviticus 18:23 calls it 'perversion'; Leviticus 20:15-16 reiterates death penalty for both human and animal; Deuteronomy 27:21 pronounces curse on violators
interpretive_questions: Does the death penalty aspect require the full Israelite judicial system including witnesses, judges, and courts as outlined elsewhere in Torah? Can this be administered outside the land of Israel? Is the moral prohibition separable from the civil penalty in terms of ongoing applicability?
notes: Death penalty for bestiality.

verse_text:
He that sacrificeth unto
any god, save unto the LORD only, he shall be utterly destroyed.
law_summary: Anyone who sacrifices to any god other than the LORD must be put to death
classification_reasoning: This is a capital punishment statute within Israel's civil/religious law code. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it addresses the fundamental issue of exclusive worship of YHVH, which is central to Torah covenant. However, execution of capital punishment appears to require established judicial authority in the land.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:20 states the prohibition and penalty directly. Deuteronomy 17:2-5 provides procedural requirements including witnesses and judges 'within thy gates.' Deuteronomy 13:6-10 shows similar capital punishment for those who entice to worship other gods.
interpretive_questions: Does this prohibition apply universally to all people or specifically to Israelites under covenant? Can the death penalty be executed without proper biblical judicial system and witnesses as outlined in Deuteronomy? Is this enforceable outside the land of Israel or without Sanhedrin-type authority? The principle of exclusive worship seems perpetual, but the execution method may require specific governmental/judicial prerequisites.
categories: LOVE_GOD > NO_IDOLATRY > Worship of False Gods
notes: Death penalty for sacrificing to any god other than the LORD.

verse_text: Thou shalt neither vex a stranger, nor oppress him: for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt.
law_summary: Do not mistreat or oppress strangers/foreigners
classification_reasoning: While lacking explicit perpetual language, this is presented as an ongoing moral statute within Torah's legal code. The rationale given ('for ye were strangers in Egypt') suggests a permanent principle based on Israel's historical experience. Multiple repetitions throughout Torah indicate fundamental, continuing importance.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:21 itself; extensive repetition in Leviticus 19:33-34 ('The stranger that dwelleth with you shall be unto you as one born among you, and thou shalt love him as thyself'); Deuteronomy 10:19 ('Love ye therefore the stranger')
interpretive_questions: Does 'stranger' (ger) refer only to resident aliens living among Israel, or include all foreigners? The Hebrew term ger can mean both sojourner/resident alien and convert/proselyte in different contexts. How does this interact with laws that distinguish between Israelites and foreigners in other contexts?
notes: Prohibition against mistreating or oppressing strangers, grounded in Israel's experience as strangers in Egypt.

verse_text: Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child.
law_summary: Do not afflict or mistreat widows or orphaned children
classification_reasoning: This is a moral/social statute given as part of the legal code without temporal limitations. The command requires no special location, priesthood, or temple service. The principle of protecting vulnerable members of society (widows and orphans) is reinforced throughout Torah and represents an ongoing ethical obligation.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:22 states simply 'Ye shall not afflict any widow, or fatherless child' with no qualifying conditions or temporal boundaries. The broader context (Exodus 22:21-27) contains similar social justice laws presented as ongoing statutes.
interpretive_questions: While lacking explicit perpetual language, the moral nature of this command and its repetition throughout Torah suggests ongoing applicability. The question remains whether this applies only to Israelites or extends to all who follow Torah principles.
categories: LOVE_NEIGHBOR > COMPASSION_AND_CARE > The Widow and Orphan
notes: Prohibition against afflicting widows and orphans.

verse_text:
If thou lend money to
any of my people
that is
 poor by thee, thou shalt not be to him as an usurer, neither shalt thou lay upon him usury.
law_summary: When lending money to poor Israelites, do not charge interest or act as a usurer
classification_reasoning: This is a moral/social statute given within the broader legal code to Israel. While no explicit perpetual language is used, the principle is restated multiple times in Torah and requires no temple infrastructure. It addresses ongoing economic relationships that can occur in any era.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:25 states the basic principle. Leviticus 25:35-37 expands it with 'that thy brother may live with thee' indicating concern for community welfare. Deuteronomy 23:19-20 distinguishes between lending to brothers vs foreigners.
interpretive_questions: Does 'my people' refer only to ethnic Israelites or include all who join Israel's covenant community? The Deuteronomy passage allows interest to foreigners but not to 'thy brother' - how does this define the scope of application?
notes: Prohibition against charging interest on loans to the poor.

verse_text: If thou at all take thy neighbour’s raiment to pledge, thou shalt deliver it unto him by that the sun goeth down:
law_summary: If you take your neighbor's garment as a pledge, you must return it to him by sunset
addressee_in_text: all Israel (thou - singular addressing the nation)
classification_reasoning: This is a civil statute governing pledge/collateral practices with no temporal limitations, prerequisites, or conditional expiration stated in the text. It appears within Torah's ongoing legal code for Israel.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:26 gives a straightforward conditional command ('If thou at all take...thou shalt deliver') with no limiting language. Deuteronomy 24:12-13 reinforces this principle, showing it as part of ongoing legal framework.
interpretive_questions: While the law itself has no expiration, practical questions remain: Does this apply only to garments specifically or to essential items more broadly? Does 'neighbor' limit application to fellow Israelites or extend to all people? The Torah text doesn't explicitly define these boundaries.
notes: A garment taken as a pledge must be returned by sunset, since it may be the person's only covering.

verse_text: Thou shalt not revile the gods, nor curse the ruler of thy people.
law_summary: Do not revile gods or curse the ruler of your people
classification_reasoning: This is a moral prohibition given as part of the legal code without temporal limitations. The command addresses fundamental issues of respect for divine authority and human leadership that transcend specific circumstances. No prerequisites are required for observance.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:28 - given as direct prohibition within the mishpatim (judgments) section following the Ten Commandments, presented as ongoing statute for Israel
interpretive_questions: The translation 'gods' (Hebrew: elohim) is ambiguous - could refer to false gods, judges, or divine beings. The scope of 'ruler of thy people' may need clarification regarding which authorities this encompasses. The relationship between not reviling 'gods' and the monotheistic framework of Torah requires consideration.
notes: Do not revile God (elohim) or curse a ruler. 'Elohim' here may refer to God or to judges. Dual categorization reflects both interpretations.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not delay
to offer the first of thy ripe fruits, and of thy liquors: the firstborn of thy sons shalt thou give unto me.
law_summary: Do not delay offering firstfruits, liquors, and firstborn sons to God
classification_reasoning: While lacking explicit perpetual language, this command appears within Torah's legal code alongside other ongoing statutes. The firstfruits and firstborn redemption system requires functioning priesthood and temple service for proper implementation.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:29 uses imperative language without temporal limitations. Related passages show this as part of Israel's ongoing covenant obligations (Deuteronomy 26:1-11 'when thou art come in unto the land'). Numbers 18:15-17 details priestly procedures for firstborn redemption.
interpretive_questions: Does 'liquors' refer to oil/wine offerings or liquid measures? Can firstfruits be offered without temple (as Abraham did)? Does firstborn redemption require formal priesthood or can any Levite suffice? The text doesn't specify consequences for delay - is this timing guidance or absolute prohibition?
notes: Do not delay offering firstfruits, liquors (first of the harvest products), and firstborn sons. Combines firstfruits/terumah obligations with the firstborn obligation.

verse_text:
Likewise shalt thou do with thine oxen,
and with thy sheep: seven days it shall be with his dam; on the eighth day thou shalt give it me.
law_summary: Young animals (oxen and sheep) must remain with their mother for seven days, then be given to God on the eighth day
classification_reasoning: This is a legal statute given to Israel without explicit temporal limitations, but it involves giving animals to God which traditionally required altar sacrifice. The command lacks explicit perpetual language but appears as an ongoing ordinance within the broader legal code.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:30 states the requirement directly as an imperative ('thou shalt'), and Leviticus 22:27 confirms this principle for acceptable sacrifices, indicating this is an established legal principle rather than a one-time command.
interpretive_questions: Does 'give it me' necessarily require formal altar sacrifice or could it mean dedication to God in other ways? The text doesn't specify the exact mechanism of giving to God, which affects current applicability without temple operations.
notes: Firstborn animals must remain with their mother for seven days and be given to God on the eighth day.

verse_text:
And ye shall be holy men unto me: neither shall ye eat
any
 flesh
that is torn of beasts in the field; ye shall cast it to the dogs.
law_summary: Do not eat flesh torn by wild beasts in the field; cast it to the dogs instead
classification_reasoning: This is a dietary law within the broader legal code with no temporal limitations stated. The command is part of ongoing holiness requirements ('ye shall be holy men unto me') and appears multiple times in Torah, indicating its ongoing nature rather than temporary application.
textual_evidence: Exodus 22:31 - 'And ye shall be holy men unto me: neither shall ye eat any flesh that is torn of beasts in the field'; Leviticus 17:15 - 'And every soul that eateth that which died of itself, or that which was torn with beasts...shall be unclean until the even'; Deuteronomy 14:21 - 'Ye shall not eat of anything that dieth of itself'
interpretive_questions: The relationship between 'torn by beasts' (treifah) and 'died of itself' (nevelah) in different passages - whether these are the same prohibition or related but distinct categories. Also, the specific instruction to 'cast it to the dogs' - whether this is prescriptive (must give to dogs) or simply descriptive of proper disposal.
notes: Prohibition against eating flesh torn by beasts (treifah). Also includes the positive command to be holy men unto God.

verse_text: Thou shalt not raise a false report: put not thine hand with the wicked to be an unrighteous witness.
law_summary: Do not spread false reports or join with the wicked as an unrighteous witness
other_torah_refs: Exodus 20:16 (ninth commandment - false witness), Leviticus 19:16 (not going about as talebearer), Deuteronomy 5:20 (false witness), Deuteronomy 19:16-19 (false witness procedures)
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental moral law about truthfulness and justice that requires no specific infrastructure, location, or role. It appears in multiple contexts including the Ten Commandments, indicating its foundational nature. The command is universal in scope and practically applicable in any time or place.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:1 gives direct prohibition; Exodus 20:16 establishes it as one of the Ten Commandments ('Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbour'); repetition across multiple Torah contexts demonstrates its ongoing importance
interpretive_questions: While clearly applicable, the specific phrase 'put not thine hand with the wicked' could raise questions about degrees of association and complicity in false witness beyond direct participation.
notes: Two prohibitions: do not spread false reports, and do not join with the wicked as an unrighteous witness.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not follow a multitude to
do evil; neither shalt thou speak in a cause to decline after many to wrest
judgment:
law_summary: Do not follow a crowd to do evil or give testimony that perverts justice by following the majority
other_torah_refs: Exodus 23:6-8 (related justice commands), Deuteronomy 16:19-20 (justice and bribery prohibitions), Leviticus 19:15 (impartial judgment)
classification_reasoning: This is a moral and judicial principle that requires no temple, priesthood, or specific location. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it appears within the covenant law code and addresses fundamental principles of justice that are inherently ongoing.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:2 - the command is given as part of the mishpatim (judgments/ordinances) section following the Ten Commandments, indicating ongoing legal statutes rather than temporary instructions
interpretive_questions: The text lacks explicit perpetual language, so classification relies on the nature of the command as a fundamental justice principle. The broader context of Exodus 21-23 contains various laws, some clearly situational and others appearing as ongoing statutes - the specific duration indicators would need to be inferred from the moral/universal nature of the prohibition.
notes: Do not follow the majority to do evil or pervert justice by following the crowd.

verse_text: Neither shalt thou countenance a poor man in his cause.
law_summary: Do not show favoritism to a poor person in a legal case
requires_specific_role: judges or those making judicial decisions
classification_reasoning: Part of judicial statutes given to Israel without time limitations. While no explicit 'forever' language, the principle is restated multiple times and forms part of ongoing justice system requirements. Requires organized judicial system but not temple/priesthood.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:1-9 contains judicial ordinances; Leviticus 19:15 'Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty'; Deuteronomy 1:17 'Ye shall not respect persons in judgment'
interpretive_questions: Does 'partial' land requirement mean this only applies in Israel proper, or wherever Israelite communities establish judicial systems? What constitutes the minimum judicial authority structure for this to apply?
notes: Do not show favoritism to the poor in legal proceedings. Leviticus 19:15 prohibits favoring both the poor and the great.

verse_text: If thou meet thine enemy’s ox or his ass going astray, thou shalt surely bring it back to him again.
law_summary: You must return your enemy's lost ox or donkey to them
addressee_in_text: Israel (second person singular addressing the nation)
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 22:1-4 (expanded version including broader obligations for lost property), Deuteronomy 22:3
classification_reasoning: This is a moral/civil statute given as part of the legal code without temporal limitations. The principle of returning lost property to enemies demonstrates ethical behavior that transcends specific historical contexts. No prerequisites are required - only the basic scenario of encountering lost property.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:4 presents this as a straightforward commandment using imperative language ('thou shalt surely bring it back'). Deuteronomy 22:1-4 reinforces and expands this principle, indicating its ongoing importance in Torah legislation.
interpretive_questions: While the specific animals mentioned (ox, ass) reflect ancient agricultural society, the question remains whether this applies only to these specific animals or represents a broader principle of returning any lost property belonging to enemies. The Deuteronomy passage suggests broader application to 'any lost thing.'
notes: Obligation to return your enemy's stray animal. Extends compassion even to one's adversary.

verse_text: If thou see the ass of him that hateth thee lying under his burden, and wouldest forbear to help him, thou shalt surely help with him.
law_summary: You must help your enemy when you see their donkey fallen under its burden, even if you would rather not help
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 22:4 - 'Thou shalt not see thy brother's ass or his ox fall down by the way, and hide thyself from them: thou shalt surely help him to lift them up again'
classification_reasoning: This is a moral/ethical statute given to Israel within the broader legal code. While it lacks explicit perpetual language, it appears as an ongoing legal obligation. The principle is reinforced in Deuteronomy with similar language, suggesting continuity. No prerequisites beyond basic social interaction are required.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:5 uses imperative language 'thou shalt surely help' indicating binding obligation. Deuteronomy 22:4 parallels this command with similar mandatory language, showing consistency across Torah's legal sections.
interpretive_questions: Does the specific mention of 'ass' limit this to that animal only, or does it represent a broader principle of helping with burden-bearing animals? The Deuteronomy passage mentions both ass and ox, suggesting broader application. Also, does 'him that hateth thee' indicate this applies beyond just enemies to all people in need?
notes: Obligation to help your enemy's donkey struggling under its burden. Extends compassion to animals and to one's adversary.

verse_text: Thou shalt not wrest the judgment of thy poor in his cause.
law_summary: Do not pervert justice for the poor in their legal cases
addressee_in_text: Israel (implied from broader context of Exodus 21-23 mishpatim)
other_torah_refs: Exodus 23:3 (do not favor poor in lawsuit), Leviticus 19:15 (judge righteously, not partial to poor or great), Deuteronomy 16:19 (do not pervert justice), Deuteronomy 27:19 (cursed who perverts justice of stranger, fatherless, widow)
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental judicial principle within the mishpatim (civil laws) section. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it represents an ongoing moral and legal standard for justice administration. The principle is reinforced multiple times throughout Torah, indicating its foundational nature.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:6 stands within the broader judicial context of Exodus 21-23. The repeated emphasis on just judgment throughout Torah (Lev 19:15, Deut 16:19) suggests this is an ongoing standard rather than time-limited instruction.
interpretive_questions: Does this apply only to formal judicial settings or to any situation where justice decisions are made? The text specifies 'thy poor' - does this indicate a community-specific application or a general principle about not letting poverty status influence justice?
notes: Do not pervert justice for the poor. Counterpart to Exodus 23:3 which prohibits favoring the poor; this prohibits discriminating against them.

verse_text: Keep thee far from a false matter; and the innocent and righteous slay thou not: for I will not justify the wicked.
law_summary: Stay away from false matters and do not kill the innocent and righteous
other_torah_refs: Exodus 20:13 (do not murder), Leviticus 19:11 (do not deal falsely), Deuteronomy 5:17 (do not murder), Deuteronomy 19:10-13 (innocent blood), Deuteronomy 27:25 (cursed is one who takes bribe to slay innocent)
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental moral and judicial principle given within the legal code to Israel. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it represents core ethical standards that require no special prerequisites and is reinforced throughout Torah.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:7 stands within the 'Book of the Covenant' legal section (Exodus 20:22-23:33). The prohibition against false matters and killing innocent/righteous aligns with fundamental commandments repeated throughout Torah, suggesting ongoing moral imperatives.
interpretive_questions: While the specific judicial context may relate to formal legal proceedings, the breadth of application (avoiding false matters generally, not harming innocent) suggests wider moral application beyond formal courts. The divine statement 'I will not justify the wicked' indicates God's ongoing moral standard rather than time-limited instruction.
notes: Contains two commands: staying away from falsehood (judicial context) and not slaying the innocent. The prohibition on killing the innocent fits under murder, while the false matter instruction relates to judicial integrity.

verse_text: And thou shalt take no gift: for the gift blindeth the wise, and perverteth the words of the righteous.
law_summary: Judges must not accept bribes or gifts, as they corrupt judgment and pervert justice
requires_specific_role: judges or those making judicial decisions
classification_reasoning: This is a fundamental principle of justice given as part of ongoing legal statutes. Though lacking explicit perpetual language, it's stated as a basic requirement for righteous judgment applicable wherever judicial decisions are made. The principle is universal in nature and requires no specific infrastructure.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:8 states the prohibition directly as part of the judicial ordinances. Deuteronomy 16:19 repeats nearly identical language: 'Thou shalt not wrest judgment; thou shalt not respect persons, neither take a gift: for a gift doth blind the eyes of the wise, and pervert the words of the righteous.'
interpretive_questions: While clearly an ongoing moral and judicial principle, the text doesn't contain explicit perpetual language. The question remains whether all judicial principles should be considered inherently ongoing based on their moral nature, or if only those with explicit perpetual language qualify as definitively eternal.

verse_text: And six years thou shalt sow thy land, and shalt gather in the fruits thereof:
law_summary: Sow your land and gather its produce for six years
other_torah_refs: Leviticus 25:3-4 (part of sabbatical year cycle), Deuteronomy 15:1 (related sabbatical year context)
classification_reasoning: This verse introduces the six-year agricultural cycle that precedes the sabbatical year. While lacking explicit perpetual language, it's part of ongoing agricultural statutes given to Israel. However, it specifically requires land ownership/cultivation in Israel.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:10 'thy land' indicates possession of agricultural land. The command is given as part of ongoing statutes to Israel, not a one-time instruction.
interpretive_questions: Does 'thy land' refer exclusively to the Land of Israel, or could it apply to any land owned by Israelites? The verse is incomplete without verse 11 which commands the sabbatical year rest, raising questions about whether this can be practiced outside the complete sabbatical system.
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Land Rest
notes: This verse establishes the six-year sowing cycle as the counterpart to the seventh-year rest commanded in the next verse.

verse_text:
But the seventh
year thou shalt let it rest and lie still; that the poor of thy people may eat: and what they leave the beasts of the field shall eat. In like manner thou shalt deal with thy vineyard,
and
 with thy oliveyard.
law_summary: In the seventh year, let the land rest and lie fallow so the poor may eat from what grows naturally, and animals may eat what remains; apply this to vineyards and olive groves as well
classification_reasoning: This is an ongoing agricultural statute given to Israel without explicit temporal limitations, but requires possession of agricultural land. The command structure ('thou shalt') indicates a continuing obligation rather than a one-time event. Related passages in Leviticus provide more detail about the same seventh-year sabbath principle.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:11 uses imperative language 'thou shalt let it rest' indicating ongoing obligation. The parallel structure 'In like manner thou shalt deal with thy vineyard, and with thy oliveyard' shows this applies broadly to agricultural holdings.
interpretive_questions: Does this apply only in the Land of Israel or wherever Israelites own agricultural land? The text doesn't specify geographic limitations, but the broader context of Torah land laws generally assumes the Promised Land. Also unclear whether this applies to individual landowners dispersed globally or requires a functioning Israelite agricultural society.
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Land Rest
LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > What Grows Freely
notes: Combines the sabbatical year land rest with provision for the poor and animals from what grows freely.

verse_text:
And in all
things that I have said unto you be circumspect: and make no mention of the name of other gods, neither let it be heard out of thy mouth.
law_summary: Do not mention the names of other gods or let them be heard from your mouth
other_torah_refs: Exodus 23:24 (do not serve their gods), Deuteronomy 12:3 (destroy names of gods from places), Joshua 23:7 (make no mention of name of their gods)
classification_reasoning: This is a moral prohibition with no time limitations, prerequisites, or conditional language. It's part of ongoing legal statutes given to Israel as a nation, addressing fundamental covenant loyalty.
textual_evidence: Exodus 23:13 'And in all things that I have said unto you be circumspect: and make no mention of the name of other gods, neither let it be heard out of thy mouth.' The command is framed as an ongoing directive within the broader legal code.
interpretive_questions: Does 'make no mention' prohibit all utterance including academic/historical reference, or specifically invocational/worshipful mention? The Hebrew context and parallel passages in Deuteronomy may provide clarity on scope.
categories: LOVE_GOD > NO_IDOLATRY > Worship of False Gods
notes: Prohibits even mentioning the names of other gods, a form of separation from idolatry.

verse_text: Three times thou shalt keep a feast unto me in the year.
law_summary: Israel must observe three feasts to God annually
