law_summary: One who unknowingly touches human uncleanness becomes guilty when they realize what happened
classification_reasoning: Purity law regarding human uncleanness contamination, no prerequisites or temporal limitations
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:3 - general principle of guilt from contact with human uncleanness
interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'uncleanness of man' without temple system to define purification?
notes: Touching human uncleanness unknowingly still incurs guilt when discovered.

verse_text:
Or if a soul swear, pronouncing with
his lips to do evil, or to do good, whatsoever
it be that a man shall pronounce with an oath, and it be hid from him; when he knoweth
of it, then he shall be guilty in one of these.
law_summary: A person who makes a rash oath and later realizes it becomes guilty and must acknowledge the sin
classification_reasoning: Moral law regarding rash oaths and their consequences, applicable without infrastructure
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:4 - universal principle about oath-making and guilt recognition
interpretive_questions: Does this apply to all forms of promises/commitments or specifically religious oaths?
notes: Rash oaths incur guilt when the person becomes aware. Covers both oaths to do evil and to do good.

verse_text:
And it shall be, when he shall be guilty in one of these
things,
 that he shall confess that he hath sinned in that
thing:
law_summary: When guilty of sin, one must confess that sin
classification_reasoning: Universal moral obligation to confess sin when guilty, no prerequisites stated
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:5 - mandatory confession 'when he shall be guilty' with no temporal or infrastructural conditions
interpretive_questions: To whom must confession be made without priesthood - directly to God or community?
notes: Requirement to confess one's sin. This is a foundational law of confession.

verse_text: And he shall bring his trespass offering unto the LORD for his sin which he hath sinned, a female from the flock, a lamb or a kid of the goats, for a sin offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him concerning his sin.
law_summary: A person who has sinned must bring a female lamb or goat as a trespass offering to the LORD, and the priest shall make atonement for the sin
classification_reasoning: Sacrificial obligation for atonement with no temporal limits, but requires priesthood and altar
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:6 - obligation to bring offering 'unto the LORD' for atonement
interpretive_questions: Is this obligation suspended or does it await restoration of proper sacrificial system?
notes: Trespass/guilt offering: female lamb or goat from the flock. Despite the verse using 'trespass offering,' it functions as an escalating sin offering in this context.

verse_text: And if he be not able to bring a lamb, then he shall bring for his trespass, which he hath committed, two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, unto the LORD; one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering.
law_summary: If unable to afford a lamb for a trespass offering, bring two turtledoves or young pigeons - one for sin offering, one for burnt offering
classification_reasoning: Accommodating provision for poor in sacrificial system, no temporal limitation but requires temple infrastructure
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:7 - conditional alternative 'if he be not able' shows ongoing concern for economic accessibility
interpretive_questions: Does this provision demonstrate eternal principle of God's accommodation for economic circumstances?
notes: Sliding-scale provision for the poor: two birds instead of a lamb for a sin/trespass offering.

verse_text:
And he shall bring them unto the priest, who shall offer
that
 which
is for the sin offering first, and wring off his head from his neck, but shall not divide
it asunder:
law_summary: The priest must offer the bird for the sin offering first, wringing off its head without completely separating it
other_torah_refs: Part of broader sacrificial system detailed throughout Leviticus
classification_reasoning: Procedural instruction for priests performing sacrificial duties - part of ongoing legal system but requires temple infrastructure
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:8 - specific procedural command for priestly sacrificial duties
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies to future temple service when prerequisites are restored
notes: Procedure for the bird sin offering: wring off head without fully separating it.

verse_text:
And he shall sprinkle of the blood of the sin offering upon the side of the altar; and the rest of the blood shall be wrung out at the bottom of the altar: it
is a sin offering.
law_summary: The priest must sprinkle blood of the sin offering on the side of the altar and wring out the remaining blood at the altar's base
other_torah_refs: Blood sprinkling procedures appear throughout Leviticus sacrifice laws
classification_reasoning: Specific procedural requirement for priestly sacrificial service - ongoing statute requiring temple and altar
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:9 - detailed procedure for blood handling in sin offerings
interpretive_questions: Application to future temple service when altar and priesthood are restored
notes: Blood handling for the bird sin offering: sprinkle on altar side, drain remainder at base.

verse_text:
And he shall offer the second
for a burnt offering, according to the manner: and the priest shall make an atonement for him for his sin which he hath sinned, and it shall be forgiven him.
law_summary: The second bird must be offered as a burnt offering according to prescribed manner, with the priest making atonement for the person's sin
other_torah_refs: Burnt offering procedures detailed throughout Leviticus 1-7
classification_reasoning: Procedural law for completing two-bird sin offering sequence - part of ongoing sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:10 - completion of prescribed sacrificial procedure for atonement
interpretive_questions: Whether future temple service will follow identical procedures
notes: The second bird offered as a burnt offering alongside the sin offering, completing the poor person's atonement.

verse_text:
But if he be not able to bring two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, then he that sinned shall bring for his offering the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a sin offering; he shall put no oil upon it, neither shall he put
any frankincense thereon: for it
is a sin offering.
law_summary: Those unable to afford birds for a sin offering must bring a tenth of an ephah of fine flour without oil or frankincense
classification_reasoning: Conditional provision for those unable to afford birds - shows ongoing concern for economic accessibility in sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:11 - conditional alternative for sin offering based on economic ability
interpretive_questions: Economic threshold determinations and application without current temple system
notes: Lowest tier of the sliding scale: flour instead of birds for the very poor. No oil or frankincense, distinguishing it from a regular grain offering.

verse_text:
Then shall he bring it to the priest, and the priest shall take his handful of it,
even a memorial thereof, and burn
it on the altar, according to the offerings made by fire unto the LORD: it
is
 a sin offering.
law_summary: The priest must take a handful of the flour offering as a memorial portion and burn it on the altar as a sin offering
other_torah_refs: Memorial portion procedures similar to grain offering laws in Leviticus 2
classification_reasoning: Priestly procedure for flour sin offering - part of ongoing sacrificial system requiring altar
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:12 - specific priestly handling of flour sin offering
interpretive_questions: Application of memorial portion concept without physical altar
notes: Procedure for the flour sin offering: priest takes a memorial handful and burns it on the altar.

verse_text:
And the priest shall make an atonement for him as touching his sin that he hath sinned in one of these, and it shall be forgiven him: and
the remnant shall be the priest’s, as a meat offering.
law_summary: The priest shall make atonement for sin offerings and the remainder belongs to the priest like a grain offering
other_torah_refs: Priestly portions detailed throughout Leviticus sacrificial laws
classification_reasoning: Establishes priestly rights to remainder of flour offering and atonement procedure - ongoing statute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:13 - priestly portion rights and atonement completion
interpretive_questions: Priestly sustenance provisions and atonement mechanism without temple
notes: Atonement through flour sin offering; remainder goes to the priest as with a grain offering.

verse_text: If a soul commit a trespass, and sin through ignorance, in the holy things of the LORD; then he shall bring for his trespass unto the LORD a ram without blemish out of the flocks, with thy estimation by shekels of silver, after the shekel of the sanctuary, for a trespass offering:
law_summary: One who unintentionally sins regarding holy things must bring a ram without blemish as a trespass offering, valued in silver shekels according to the sanctuary standard
other_torah_refs: Trespass offerings detailed in Leviticus 7:1-10, Leviticus 6:6, Leviticus 5:18
classification_reasoning: Obligation for unintentional sins against holy things - ongoing moral and ritual requirement
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:15 - specific requirement for trespass offering with monetary valuation
interpretive_questions: Application of sanctuary shekel standard and holy things definition without temple
notes: Guilt offering for unintentional misuse of holy things: unblemished ram valued in silver shekels.

verse_text: And he shall make amends for the harm that he hath done in the holy thing, and shall add the fifth part thereto, and give it unto the priest: and the priest shall make an atonement for him with the ram of the trespass offering, and it shall be forgiven him.
law_summary: One who damages holy things must make full restitution plus an additional fifth, give it to the priest, and bring a ram for a trespass offering
other_torah_refs: Restitution plus fifth principle appears in Numbers 5:7
classification_reasoning: Restitution law for damages to holy things - combines civil and ritual obligations in ongoing system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:16 - full restitution plus fifth to priest with ram offering
interpretive_questions: Identification of holy things and priestly recipients without functioning temple system
notes: Restitution for damage to holy things: full restitution plus one-fifth, given to the priest, plus the ram guilt offering.

verse_text:
And if a soul sin, and commit any of these things which are forbidden to be done by the commandments of the LORD; though he wist
it not, yet is he guilty, and shall bear his iniquity.
law_summary: One who unknowingly violates any of the LORD's commandments is still guilty and must bear their iniquity
other_torah_refs: Principle of guilt for unknowing sin echoed throughout Torah
classification_reasoning: Fundamental principle that ignorance does not excuse violation of God's commandments - moral law
textual_evidence: Leviticus 5:17 - establishes guilt and responsibility regardless of knowledge
interpretive_questions: Scope of 'commandments of the LORD' and practical implications of bearing iniquity
notes: Establishes principle that ignorance does not eliminate guilt for violating God's commands.

verse_text: Or have found that which was lost, and lieth concerning it, and sweareth falsely; in any of all these that a man doeth, sinning therein:
law_summary: Prohibition against lying under oath about finding lost property
classification_reasoning: Moral prohibition against false oaths with no temporal limitations or prerequisites
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:3 - applies to 'any of all these that a man doeth'
interpretive_questions: None - clear moral prohibition
notes: Lying under oath about found property. Dual categorization: false oath and property fraud.

verse_text: Then it shall be, because he hath sinned, and is guilty, that he shall restore that which he took violently away, or the thing which he hath deceitfully gotten, or that which was delivered him to keep, or the lost thing which he found,
law_summary: A person who has sinned by theft, fraud, or withholding must restore the stolen or wrongfully obtained property
classification_reasoning: Civil restitution law with no temporal limitations, part of ongoing justice system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:4 - 'he shall restore' - ongoing obligation for wrongdoing
interpretive_questions: None - clear restitution requirement
notes: Restitution requirement for stolen, defrauded, or wrongfully withheld property.

verse_text:
Or all that about which he hath sworn falsely; he shall even restore it in the principal, and shall add the fifth part more thereto,
and
 give it unto him to whom it appertaineth, in the day of his trespass offering.
law_summary: One who has sworn falsely must restore the principal amount plus one-fifth to the rightful owner on the day of the trespass offering
classification_reasoning: Restitution principle ongoing, but tied to 'day of trespass offering' requiring temple system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:5 - 'in the day of his trespass offering' links to temple requirement
interpretive_questions: Whether restitution+20% applies without temple, or only the timing is temple-dependent
notes: Restitution of principal plus one-fifth for false oaths regarding property, to be paid on the day of the trespass offering.

verse_text: And the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD: and it shall be forgiven him for any thing of all that he hath done in trespassing therein.
law_summary: The priest shall make atonement for a person who has trespassed, and they will be forgiven
classification_reasoning: Ongoing priestly duty requiring functional temple and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:7 - 'the priest shall make atonement' - requires active priesthood
interpretive_questions: None - clearly requires functioning priesthood
notes: Priestly atonement for the trespass, resulting in forgiveness.

verse_text:
Command Aaron and his sons, saying, This
is the law of the burnt offering: It
is the burnt offering, because of the burning upon the altar all night unto the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be burning in it.
law_summary: The burnt offering must remain burning on the altar all night until morning, and the altar fire must be kept burning continuously
classification_reasoning: Ongoing sacrificial procedure requiring functional altar and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:9 - 'burning upon the altar' and 'fire of the altar' - requires temple altar
interpretive_questions: None - clearly requires temple altar and priesthood
notes: Law of the burnt offering: must remain burning all night until morning. Altar fire kept continuously burning.

verse_text: And the priest shall put on his linen garment, and his linen breeches shall he put upon his flesh, and take up the ashes which the fire hath consumed with the burnt offering on the altar, and he shall put them beside the altar.
law_summary: The priest must wear linen garments and linen undergarments when removing ashes from burnt offerings on the altar
classification_reasoning: Ongoing priestly procedure requiring functional altar and temple service
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:10 - 'take up the ashes...from the altar' - requires temple altar
interpretive_questions: None - clearly requires functioning temple altar
notes: Priestly duty of ash removal; also specifies priestly garments (linen) for this task. Dual categorization warranted.

verse_text: And he shall put off his garments, and put on other garments, and carry forth the ashes without the camp unto a clean place.
law_summary: The priest must change into other garments and carry the ashes from burnt offerings to a clean place outside the camp
classification_reasoning: Ongoing priestly duty requiring temple service and proper disposal location
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:11 - 'carry forth the ashes without the camp' - requires temple context
interpretive_questions: Whether 'camp' refers to temporary wilderness or permanent temple context
notes: Priest must change garments to carry ashes outside the camp to a clean place.

verse_text: And the fire upon the altar shall be burning in it; it shall not be put out: and the priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and lay the burnt offering in order upon it; and he shall burn thereon the fat of the peace offerings.
law_summary: The altar fire must be kept burning continuously and the priest must add wood each morning, arrange burnt offerings, and burn the fat of peace offerings
classification_reasoning: Ongoing priestly obligation requiring functional altar and continuous temple service
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:12 - 'fire upon the altar' and 'priest shall burn wood' - requires temple altar
interpretive_questions: None - clearly requires functioning temple altar
notes: Perpetual fire on the altar; priest's daily duty to add wood and arrange offerings each morning. The 'fire shall not be put out' is a significant perpetual command.

verse_text:
And this
is the law of the meat offering: the sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, before the altar.
law_summary: The sons of Aaron (priests) must offer the grain offering before the LORD at the altar
addressee_in_text: general instruction about sons of Aaron
other_torah_refs: Multiple references to grain offerings throughout Leviticus
classification_reasoning: Stated as 'the law' but no explicit perpetual language; requires altar and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:14 - 'this is the law of the meat offering'
interpretive_questions: Whether 'the law' implies permanence without explicit perpetual language
notes: Introduction to the law of the grain offering: sons of Aaron offer it before the LORD at the altar.

verse_text: And the remainder thereof shall Aaron and his sons eat: with unleavened bread shall it be eaten in the holy place; in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation they shall eat it.
law_summary: Aaron and his sons must eat the remainder of the grain offering with unleavened bread in the holy place, specifically in the court of the tabernacle
other_torah_refs: Priestly portions mentioned throughout Leviticus, Leviticus 10:12, Numbers 18:10
classification_reasoning: Ongoing obligation without explicit duration; requires tabernacle/temple court
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:16 - 'shall eat' in holy place
interpretive_questions: Whether tabernacle court requirement extends to temple court
notes: Remainder of grain offerings: eaten unleavened by Aaron and sons in the holy place (tabernacle court).

verse_text:
It shall not be baken with leaven. I have given it
unto them for
 their portion of my offerings made by fire; it
is most holy, as
is the sin offering, and as the trespass offering.
law_summary: The priests' portion of grain offerings must not be baked with leaven
classification_reasoning: Prohibition without explicit duration; applies when grain offerings are made
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:17 - 'It shall not be baken with leaven'
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies to all priestly grain preparation or only offerings
notes: Priestly portion of grain offerings must not be baked with leaven. Designated as most holy.

verse_text:
All the males among the children of Aaron shall eat of it.
It shall be a statute for ever in your generations concerning the offerings of the LORD made by fire: every one that toucheth them shall be holy.
law_summary: Male descendants of Aaron shall eat of the fire offerings, and anyone who touches these offerings becomes holy
forever_phrase: It shall be a statute for ever
requires_specific_role: males among the children of Aaron
addressee_in_text: males among the children of Aaron
classification_reasoning: Explicit perpetual language 'statute for ever in your generations' regarding fire offerings
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:18 - 'It shall be a statute for ever in your generations concerning the offerings of the LORD made by fire'
interpretive_questions: Whether holiness by touching applies beyond active temple service
notes: Male descendants of Aaron eat of fire offerings as a perpetual statute. Contagious holiness: whatever touches them becomes holy.

verse_text:
This
is the offering of Aaron and of his sons, which they shall offer unto the LORD in the day when he is anointed; the tenth part of an ephah of fine flour for a meat offering perpetual, half of it in the morning, and half thereof at night.
law_summary: Aaron and his sons must offer a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a perpetual grain offering, half in the morning and half at night, on the day of anointing
classification_reasoning: Uses 'perpetual' (tamid) for the offering itself, indicating continuous nature
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:20 - 'perpetual' meat offering, half morning and half night
interpretive_questions: Whether 'perpetual' applies to the offering schedule or the law itself
notes: The high priest's perpetual grain offering: a tenth of an ephah, half morning and half evening. Connected to priestly anointing.

verse_text:
In a pan it shall be made with oil;
and when it is baken, thou shalt bring it in:
and the baken pieces of the meat offering shalt thou offer
for a sweet savour unto the LORD.
law_summary: The meat offering must be prepared in a pan with oil, baked, and offered in pieces as a sweet savor to the LORD
classification_reasoning: Procedural instruction without explicit duration; part of perpetual offering context
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:21 - specific preparation method with 'shall' language
interpretive_questions: Whether this specific pan method is required for all similar offerings
notes: Preparation method for the priestly grain offering: made in a pan with oil, baked, offered in pieces.

verse_text:
And the priest of his sons that is anointed in his stead shall offer it:
it is a statute for ever unto the LORD, it shall be wholly burnt.
law_summary: The anointed priest among Aaron's sons who succeeds him must offer the grain offering, and it must be completely burned as a perpetual statute
forever_phrase: it is a statute for ever unto the LORD
addressee_in_text: the priest of his sons that is anointed
classification_reasoning: Explicit 'statute for ever unto the LORD' regarding priestly succession and complete burning
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:22 - 'it is a statute for ever unto the LORD, it shall be wholly burnt'
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies only to succession offerings or all priestly grain offerings
notes: The successor high priest must offer this grain offering as a perpetual statute; it must be completely burned.

verse_text: For every meat offering for the priest shall be wholly burnt: it shall not be eaten.
law_summary: All grain offerings made by priests must be completely burned and cannot be eaten
classification_reasoning: Universal prohibition for priestly grain offerings without explicit duration
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:23 - 'For every meat offering for the priest shall be wholly burnt: it shall not be eaten'
interpretive_questions: Whether 'every' extends this rule beyond the immediate context
notes: Priestly grain offerings must be completely burned, not eaten. Distinguishes priestly grain offerings from those of laypersons.

verse_text:
Speak unto Aaron and to his sons, saying, This
is the law of the sin offering: In the place where the burnt offering is killed shall the sin offering be killed before the LORD: it
is most holy.
law_summary: The sin offering must be killed in the same place where the burnt offering is killed, before the LORD
classification_reasoning: Stated as 'the law of the sin offering' without explicit duration; requires sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:25 - 'This is the law of the sin offering'
interpretive_questions: Whether location requirements are absolute or contextual to the tabernacle/temple
notes: Law of the sin offering: killed in the same place as the burnt offering. Designated most holy.

verse_text: The priest that offereth it for sin shall eat it: in the holy place shall it be eaten, in the court of the tabernacle of the congregation.
law_summary: The priest who offers the sin offering must eat it in the holy place, specifically in the court of the tabernacle
requires_specific_role: priest that offereth it for sin
other_torah_refs: Leviticus 6:14-18 broader sin offering context, Leviticus 10:18
classification_reasoning: Part of sacrificial system instructions given as ongoing legal statute, but requires functioning tabernacle/temple and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:26 - specifies location as 'court of the tabernacle of the congregation' and role as 'priest that offereth it'
interpretive_questions: Whether 'holy place' could refer to other locations if tabernacle/temple unavailable
notes: The officiating priest must eat the sin offering in the holy place (tabernacle court).

verse_text: Whatsoever shall touch the flesh thereof shall be holy: and when there is sprinkled of the blood thereof upon any garment, thou shalt wash that whereon it was sprinkled in the holy place.
law_summary: Whatever touches the flesh of the sin offering becomes holy, and garments sprinkled with its blood must be washed in the holy place
classification_reasoning: Holiness transfer principle for sin offerings requires active sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:27 - washing must occur 'in the holy place' indicating temple/tabernacle requirement
interpretive_questions: Scope of holiness transfer principle outside sacrificial context
notes: Contagious holiness of sin offering flesh; blood-sprinkled garments must be washed in the holy place.

verse_text: But the earthen vessel wherein it is sodden shall be broken: and if it be sodden in a brasen pot, it shall be both scoured, and rinsed in water.
law_summary: Earthen vessels used for cooking sin offerings must be broken; bronze vessels must be scoured and rinsed with water
classification_reasoning: Vessel purification law specific to sin offering preparation in sacred context
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:28 - refers to vessels used for cooking sin offerings
interpretive_questions: Whether principle applies to other holy food preparation
notes: Vessel purification after cooking sin offerings: earthen vessels broken, bronze vessels scoured and rinsed.

verse_text:
All the males among the priests shall eat thereof: it
is
 most holy.
law_summary: Male priests must eat the sin offering as it is most holy
classification_reasoning: Priestly obligation requiring active sin offering system and functioning priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:29 - specifies 'males among the priests' and 'most holy' status
interpretive_questions: Gender restriction rationale and broader application
notes: Only male priests may eat the sin offering; it is most holy.

verse_text:
And no sin offering, whereof
any of the blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile
withal in the holy
place, shall be eaten: it shall be burnt in the fire.
law_summary: Sin offerings whose blood is brought into the tabernacle for atonement in the holy place must not be eaten but burned with fire
classification_reasoning: Distinguishes between types of sin offerings based on blood ritual location
textual_evidence: Leviticus 6:30 - 'blood is brought into the tabernacle of the congregation to reconcile withal in the holy place'
interpretive_questions: Distinction between different classes of sin offerings
notes: Exception: sin offerings whose blood is brought into the tabernacle for inner-sanctuary atonement must be burned entirely, not eaten.

verse_text:
Likewise this
is the law of the trespass offering: it
is most holy.
law_summary: The trespass offering is designated as most holy
classification_reasoning: Foundational designation for trespass offering requiring sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:1 - 'this is the law of the trespass offering: it is most holy'
interpretive_questions: Implications of 'most holy' designation
notes: Introduction to the law of the trespass/guilt offering: designated most holy.

verse_text: In the place where they kill the burnt offering shall they kill the trespass offering: and the blood thereof shall he sprinkle round about upon the altar.
law_summary: The trespass offering must be killed in the same place as the burnt offering, and its blood sprinkled around the altar
classification_reasoning: Procedural requirement for trespass offering location and blood ritual
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:2 - links location to burnt offering place and specifies altar blood sprinkling
interpretive_questions: Significance of shared location with burnt offering
notes: Guilt offering killed at the same location as the burnt offering; blood sprinkled around the altar.

verse_text: And he shall offer of it all the fat thereof; the rump, and the fat that covereth the inwards,
law_summary: All fat from the rump and covering the internal organs must be offered in sacrifice
classification_reasoning: Specific anatomical requirements for trespass offering fat portions
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:3 - 'he shall offer of it all the fat thereof; the rump, and the fat that covereth the inwards'
interpretive_questions: Precise anatomical identification of required fat portions
notes: Fat portions of the guilt offering that must be offered.

verse_text:
And the priest shall burn them upon the altar
for an offering made by fire unto the LORD: it
is a trespass offering.
law_summary: Priests must burn the fat portions of the trespass offering on the altar as a fire offering to the LORD
other_torah_refs: General altar fire offering procedures throughout Leviticus
classification_reasoning: Final step in trespass offering procedure requiring altar and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:5 - 'priest shall burn them upon the altar for an offering made by fire unto the LORD'
interpretive_questions: Relationship between fire offering and atonement completion
notes: Priests burn the fat portions of the guilt offering on the altar.

verse_text:
Every male among the priests shall eat thereof: it shall be eaten in the holy place: it
is most holy.
law_summary: Male priests must eat the guilt offering in the holy place, as it is most holy
other_torah_refs: Part of broader priestly instruction context
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning priesthood and holy place (temple/tabernacle) to execute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:6 - 'it shall be eaten in the holy place'
interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'holy place' without temple? Can Aaronic priesthood function without temple?
notes: Male priests eat the guilt offering in the holy place; it is most holy.

verse_text:
As the sin offering
is, so
is the trespass offering:
there is one law for them: the priest that maketh atonement therewith shall have
it.
law_summary: The priest who performs atonement with sin offerings and trespass offerings shall receive them as his portion
other_torah_refs: Connected to broader sacrificial system throughout Leviticus
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning sacrificial system with sin and trespass offerings
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:7 - 'there is one law for them'
interpretive_questions: Does 'one law' imply perpetuity? How does this apply without temple sacrifices?
notes: Sin and guilt offerings follow the same law; the officiating priest receives them.

verse_text:
And the priest that offereth any man’s burnt offering,
even
 the priest shall have to himself the skin of the burnt offering which he hath offered.
law_summary: The priest who offers a burnt offering receives the skin of the animal as his portion
other_torah_refs: Part of burnt offering regulations throughout Torah
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning sacrificial system and priesthood for burnt offerings
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:8 - 'the priest that offereth any man's burnt offering'
interpretive_questions: Can burnt offerings be made without temple? What constitutes valid priesthood?
notes: The priest who offers a burnt offering receives the animal's skin as his portion.

verse_text: And all the meat offering that is baken in the oven, and all that is dressed in the fryingpan, and in the pan, shall be the priest’s that offereth it.
law_summary: All grain offerings baked in an oven, cooked in a pan, or prepared in a frying pan belong to the priest who offers them
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning sacrificial system for grain offerings
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:9 - 'shall be the priest's that offereth it'
interpretive_questions: Can grain offerings be made independently of temple system?
notes: Cooked grain offerings belong to the officiating priest.

verse_text:
And every meat offering, mingled with oil, and dry, shall all the sons of Aaron have, one
as much as another.
law_summary: All sons of Aaron (priests) shall equally share in every grain offering, whether mixed with oil or dry
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning Aaronic priesthood and grain offering system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:10 - 'shall all the sons of Aaron have'
interpretive_questions: Does equal sharing principle apply to all priestly functions? How are sons of Aaron identified today?
notes: Uncooked grain offerings (with oil or dry) are shared equally among all sons of Aaron.

verse_text:
And this
is the law of the sacrifice of peace offerings, which he shall offer unto the LORD.
law_summary: Introduction to the law governing peace offerings to be presented to the LORD
other_torah_refs: Peace offering regulations throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy
classification_reasoning: Introductory verse establishing law framework for peace offerings requiring sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:11 - 'this is the law of the sacrifice of peace offerings'
interpretive_questions: Is this merely introductory or does it establish perpetual framework?
notes: Introduction to the detailed law of peace offerings.

verse_text: If he offer it for a thanksgiving, then he shall offer with the sacrifice of thanksgiving unleavened cakes mingled with oil, and unleavened wafers anointed with oil, and cakes mingled with oil, of fine flour, fried.
law_summary: When offering a thanksgiving sacrifice, specific unleavened bread offerings must be included: cakes mingled with oil, wafers anointed with oil, and fried cakes of fine flour with oil
other_torah_refs: Thanksgiving offering context in Psalms and other locations
classification_reasoning: Conditional law ('If he offer it') requiring functioning sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:12 - 'If he offer it for a thanksgiving'
interpretive_questions: Can thanksgiving offerings be made without temple? What constitutes proper thanksgiving sacrifice?
notes: Thanksgiving peace offering: specific bread accompaniments required (unleavened cakes with oil, wafers, fried cakes).

verse_text:
Besides the cakes, he shall offer
for his offering leavened bread with the sacrifice of thanksgiving of his peace offerings.
law_summary: Leavened bread must be offered alongside cakes as part of the thanksgiving sacrifice of peace offerings
other_torah_refs: Leaven regulations in Exodus and elsewhere
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning sacrificial system for thanksgiving peace offerings
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:13 - 'with the sacrifice of thanksgiving of his peace offerings'
interpretive_questions: Why is leavened bread permitted here when usually forbidden in offerings?
notes: Leavened bread must also accompany the thanksgiving peace offering. Notable because leaven is generally prohibited in fire offerings.

verse_text:
And of it he shall offer one out of the whole oblation
for
 an heave offering unto the LORD,
and it shall be the priest’s that sprinkleth the blood of the peace offerings.
law_summary: One portion from the oblation must be offered as a heave offering to the LORD and given to the priest who sprinkles the blood of peace offerings
classification_reasoning: Requires functioning priesthood and sacrificial blood sprinkling procedures
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:14 - 'the priest's that sprinkleth the blood'
interpretive_questions: How is the 'one out of the whole oblation' determined? What constitutes proper heave offering?
notes: One portion from each type of bread offered as a heave offering to the LORD; goes to the priest who sprinkles the blood.

verse_text: And the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings for thanksgiving shall be eaten the same day that it is offered; he shall not leave any of it until the morning.
law_summary: The meat from thanksgiving peace offerings must be eaten on the same day it is offered and not left until morning
other_torah_refs: Timing regulations for sacrificial consumption throughout Leviticus
classification_reasoning: Requires thanksgiving peace offering system to be operative
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:15 - 'shall be eaten the same day that it is offered'
interpretive_questions: Does this timing principle apply to other contexts? Why the strict same-day requirement for thanksgiving specifically?
notes: Thanksgiving peace offering meat must be eaten the same day; none left until morning.

verse_text:
But if the sacrifice of his offering
be a vow, or a voluntary offering, it shall be eaten the same day that he offereth his sacrifice: and on the morrow also the remainder of it shall be eaten:
law_summary: Vow and voluntary offerings must be eaten on the day offered and the day after, with any remainder consumed by the second day
classification_reasoning: Part of sacrificial system requiring temple infrastructure; no explicit temporal limitations given
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:16 - procedural instruction for vow and voluntary offerings
interpretive_questions: Could this apply to future temple service or other sacrificial contexts?
notes: Vow and voluntary peace offerings may be eaten on the day offered and the following day.

verse_text: But the remainder of the flesh of the sacrifice on the third day shall be burnt with fire.
law_summary: Any remaining flesh from a sacrifice must be burned with fire on the third day
classification_reasoning: Sacrificial procedure with no temporal limitations; requires functioning sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:17 - mandatory burning of sacrifice remainder on third day
interpretive_questions: Would this apply to any future sacrificial contexts outside temple system?
notes: Remaining sacrifice flesh must be burned on the third day.

verse_text:
And if
any of the flesh of the sacrifice of his peace offerings be eaten at all on the third day, it shall not be accepted, neither shall it be imputed unto him that offereth it: it shall be an abomination, and the soul that eateth of it shall bear his iniquity.
law_summary: Peace offering meat eaten on the third day is forbidden and makes the offering invalid
classification_reasoning: Prohibition regarding peace offerings with no stated time limit; requires sacrificial system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:18 - eating peace offering meat on third day makes offering invalid and is abomination
interpretive_questions: Does the 'abomination' status suggest this principle extends beyond temple context?
notes: Eating peace offering meat on the third day invalidates the entire offering and makes it an abomination (piggul).

verse_text:
And the flesh that toucheth any unclean
thing shall not be eaten; it shall be burnt with fire: and as for the flesh, all that be clean shall eat thereof.
law_summary: Flesh that touches anything unclean must not be eaten but burned with fire; only ritually clean people may eat clean flesh
classification_reasoning: Purity law with broader application beyond sacrifices; no temporal limitations given
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:19 - unclean flesh not eaten, burned with fire; only clean people eat clean flesh
interpretive_questions: Could purity principles apply to any ritual flesh consumption outside sacrificial context?
notes: Offering flesh contaminated by unclean contact must be burned; only clean persons may eat clean flesh. Intersects offering law and purity.

verse_text:
But the soul that eateth
of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, that
pertain unto the LORD, having his uncleanness upon him, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.
law_summary: An unclean person who eats from peace offerings shall be cut off from the people
classification_reasoning: Severe penalty (cut off) suggests ongoing importance; requires peace offering system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:20 - unclean person eating peace offerings cut off from people
interpretive_questions: Does 'cut off' penalty indicate this principle extends beyond temple period?
notes: Unclean person eating peace offerings: cut off from the people.

verse_text:
Moreover the soul that shall touch any unclean
thing, as
 the uncleanness of man, or
any unclean beast, or any abominable unclean
thing, and eat of the flesh of the sacrifice of peace offerings, which
pertain unto the LORD, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.
law_summary: Anyone who touches something unclean and then eats peace offering meat shall be cut off from their people
classification_reasoning: Purity law for peace offerings with severe penalty; no temporal limitations stated
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:21 - touching unclean thing then eating peace offering meat results in being cut off
interpretive_questions: Are the purity principles separable from the sacrificial context?
notes: Touching unclean things and then eating peace offerings: cut off. Expands the scope of contamination sources.

verse_text: And the fat of the beast that dieth of itself, and the fat of that which is torn with beasts, may be used in any other use: but ye shall in no wise eat of it.
law_summary: The fat of animals that died naturally or were torn by wild animals may be used for other purposes but must not be eaten
classification_reasoning: Dietary law distinguishing permitted uses from consumption; no prerequisites or temporal limits
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:24 - fat of naturally dead or torn animals may be used but not eaten
interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'any other use' for such fat?
notes: Fat of naturally dead or torn animals may be used for non-food purposes but must not be eaten.

verse_text:

interpretive_questions: Does this only apply when fire offerings are actually being made, or to designated animals?
notes: Eating fat of animals offered as fire offerings results in being cut off. Penalty clause for the fat prohibition.

verse_text:
Whatsoever soul
it be that eateth any manner of blood, even that soul shall be cut off from his people.

interpretive_questions: None - clear universal prohibition

verse_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, He that offereth the sacrifice of his peace offerings unto the LORD shall bring his oblation unto the LORD of the sacrifice of his peace offerings.

notes: The offerer must personally bring the oblation from his peace offering sacrifice to the LORD.

verse_text:
His own hands shall bring the offerings of the LORD made by fire, the fat with the breast, it shall he bring, that the breast may be waved
for a wave offering before the LORD.
law_summary: The offerer must personally bring the fat and breast of peace offerings to be waved before the LORD
classification_reasoning: Specific procedure for peace offerings requiring wave offering before the LORD at sanctuary
textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:30 specifies waving 'before the LORD' indicating temple requirement
interpretive_questions: Personal bringing requirement vs. representative bringing
notes: The offerer must personally bring the fat and breast, with the breast waved as a wave offering before the LORD.

verse_text: And the priest shall burn the fat upon the altar: but the breast shall be Aaron’s and his sons’.

textual_evidence: Leviticus 7:31 specifies priest's role at altar and priestly inheritance
interpretive_questions: Application when priesthood and altar are restored
notes: Fat burned on the altar; breast goes to Aaron and his sons as their portion.

for

verse_text:
And Moses said unto Aaron and to his sons, Boil the flesh at the door of the tabernacle of the congregation: and there eat it with the bread that
is in the basket of consecrations, as I commanded, saying, Aaron and his sons shall eat it.
law_summary: Aaron and his sons must boil and eat the consecration flesh at the tabernacle door along with the bread from the basket of consecrations

verse_text:

verse_text:

verse_text: And Moses said unto Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons, Uncover not your heads, neither rend your clothes; lest ye die, and lest wrath come upon all the people: but let your brethren, the whole house of Israel, bewail the burning which the LORD hath kindled.

addressee_in_text: Aaron, and unto Eleazar and unto Ithamar, his sons
classification_reasoning: Specific instruction given during mourning for Nadab and Abihu, no explicit perpetual language but implies ongoing priestly conduct rules
textual_evidence: Leviticus 10:6 - addressed to specific priests during specific event
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies to all future priestly mourning or was specific to this incident
notes: Though given in the context of Nadab and Abihu's death, the prohibition on priests uncovering heads or rending garments during mourning is an ongoing priestly restriction. Leviticus 21:10 confirms this as permanent law for the high priest.

verse_text:
And ye shall not go out from the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, lest ye die: for the anointing oil of the LORD
is
 upon you. And they did according to the word of Moses.

verse_text: And that ye may put difference between holy and unholy, and between unclean and clean;

verse_text: Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If a woman have conceived seed, and born a man child: then she shall be unclean seven days; according to the days of the separation for her infirmity shall she be unclean.

verse_text: And she shall then continue in the blood of her purifying three and thirty days; she shall touch no hallowed thing, nor come into the sanctuary, until the days of her purifying be fulfilled.

verse_text: But if she bear a maid child, then she shall be unclean two weeks, as in her separation: and she shall continue in the blood of her purifying threescore and six days.

verse_text: And when the days of her purifying are fulfilled, for a son, or for a daughter, she shall bring a lamb of the first year for a burnt offering, and a young pigeon, or a turtledove, for a sin offering, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, unto the priest:

law_summary: A man who is bald from natural hair loss is ritually clean
classification_reasoning: Direct statement about ritual cleanliness of natural baldness, requires no special prerequisites
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:40 - clear statement that natural baldness does not cause uncleanness
interpretive_questions: None - straightforward declaration of clean status
notes: Natural baldness does not constitute impurity

verse_text:
And he that hath his hair fallen off from the part of his head toward his face, he
is forehead bald:
yet is he clean.
law_summary: A man who is bald on the front of his head is ritually clean
classification_reasoning: Direct statement about ritual cleanliness of natural frontal baldness, requires no special prerequisites
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:41 - clear statement that frontal baldness does not cause uncleanness
interpretive_questions: None - straightforward declaration of clean status
notes: Forehead baldness does not constitute impurity

law_summary: The priest must examine a white-reddish sore on a bald head or forehead to determine if it appears like leprosy on skin
other_torah_refs: Part of broader skin condition laws in Leviticus 13
classification_reasoning: Priestly diagnostic procedure within ongoing purity system, no explicit perpetual language but part of continuing statute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:43 - priestly examination of sores on bald heads
interpretive_questions: How should this diagnostic process function without established priesthood?

verse_text:
He is a leprous man, he
is unclean: the priest shall pronounce him utterly unclean; his plague
is in his head.
law_summary: A priest must pronounce a person with leprosy in the head as utterly unclean
other_torah_refs: Part of broader leprosy laws in Leviticus 13
classification_reasoning: Priestly obligation within ongoing purity system, no explicit perpetual language but part of continuing statute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:44 - mandatory priestly declaration of uncleanness for head leprosy
interpretive_questions: What authority structure should handle these declarations in absence of functioning priesthood?

verse_text:
And the leper in whom the plague
is, his clothes shall be rent, and his head bare, and he shall put a covering upon his upper lip, and shall cry, Unclean, unclean.
law_summary: A person with leprosy must tear their clothes, uncover their head, cover their upper lip, and cry out 'Unclean, unclean'

interpretive_questions: Does this apply to all skin conditions or only to specific biblical leprosy? How is initial diagnosis made?
notes: Behavioral requirements for the leper: torn clothes, bare head, covered lip, cry 'Unclean'

verse_text:
All the days wherein the plague
shall be in him he shall be defiled; he
is unclean: he shall dwell alone; without the camp
shall his habitation
be.
law_summary: A person with a plague must remain defiled, dwell alone, and live outside the camp for as long as the plague is present
classification_reasoning: Ongoing isolation requirement for those with plagues, though 'camp' structure may require adaptation to current contexts
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:46 - isolation requirements for duration of plague condition
interpretive_questions: How does 'outside the camp' translate to modern community structures? What constitutes proper isolation?
categories: LOVE_GOD > PURITY_AND_HOLINESS > Holiness of the Camp
notes: Leper must dwell outside the camp; intersects with camp holiness laws

verse_text:
And if the plague be greenish or reddish in the garment, or in the skin, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin; it
is
 a plague of leprosy, and shall be shewed unto the priest:
law_summary: Items with greenish or reddish discoloration must be shown to the priest for leprosy examination
other_torah_refs: Part of broader leprosy laws in Leviticus 13-14
classification_reasoning: Part of ongoing purity statutes but requires functioning priesthood for examination

interpretive_questions: Whether non-Aaronic priests could perform this function; modern application without temple system
notes: Tzaraat of garments; greenish or reddish discoloration must be shown to priest

verse_text:
And the priest shall look upon the plague, and shut up
it that hath the plague seven days:
law_summary: The priest must examine a plague and quarantine the affected item for seven days
other_torah_refs: Part of priestly procedures in Leviticus 13-14
classification_reasoning: Procedural requirement for priests as part of ongoing purity system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:50 - 'the priest shall look upon the plague, and shut up'
interpretive_questions: Application without functioning Aaronic priesthood

verse_text:
And he shall look on the plague on the seventh day: if the plague be spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in a skin,
or in any work that is made of skin; the plague
is a fretting leprosy; it
is unclean.
law_summary: A priest must examine a suspected plague in fabric or leather on the seventh day, and if it has spread, it must be declared unclean as a fretting leprosy
classification_reasoning: Ongoing procedural law for priestly examination of suspected leprosy
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:51 - 'he shall look on the plague on the seventh day'
interpretive_questions: Modern application of leprosy identification and declaration procedures

verse_text:
He shall therefore burn that garment, whether warp or woof, in woollen or in linen, or any thing of skin, wherein the plague is: for it
is
 a fretting leprosy; it shall be burnt in the fire.
law_summary: Garments or leather items with leprous plague must be burned in fire
classification_reasoning: Ongoing statute requiring priestly determination before destruction
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:52 - 'it shall be burnt in the fire' following priestly examination
interpretive_questions: Whether destruction can occur without priestly authority

verse_text: And if the priest shall look, and, behold, the plague be not spread in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin;
law_summary: The priest must examine clothing or leather items for plague spread
classification_reasoning: Priestly procedural requirement as part of ongoing purity system
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:53 - 'if the priest shall look' indicates ongoing priestly duty
interpretive_questions: Incomplete verse requires context from surrounding verses

verse_text:
Then the priest shall command that they wash
the thing wherein the plague
is, and he shall shut it up seven days more:
law_summary: The priest must command washing of items with plague marks and isolate them for seven more days
classification_reasoning: Ongoing priestly procedure for potential purification of suspected items
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:54 - 'the priest shall command' shows ongoing authority
interpretive_questions: Modern application of quarantine and cleansing procedures

verse_text:
And the priest shall look on the plague, after that it is washed: and, behold,
if the plague have not changed his colour, and the plague be not spread; it
is unclean; thou shalt burn it in the fire; it
is fret inward,
whether it
be bare within or without.
law_summary: If a plague on clothing does not change color or spread after washing, the priest must declare it unclean and it must be burned
other_torah_refs: Leviticus 13 conditional procedures for unclean items
classification_reasoning: Conditional ongoing law requiring priestly examination and declaration
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:55 - 'the priest shall look' and 'thou shalt burn it'
interpretive_questions: Authority to execute destruction without functioning priesthood

verse_text:
And if the priest look, and, behold, the plague
be somewhat dark after the washing of it; then he shall rend it out of the garment, or out of the skin, or out of the warp, or out of the woof:
law_summary: If a plague spot becomes dim after washing, the priest must tear out the affected portion from the garment or material
classification_reasoning: Ongoing conditional procedure requiring priestly examination and action
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:56 - 'if the priest look' and 'he shall rend it out'
interpretive_questions: Whether partial remediation can be performed without priestly oversight

verse_text:
And if it appear still in the garment, either in the warp, or in the woof, or in any thing of skin; it
is a spreading
plague:
 thou shalt burn that wherein the plague
is with fire.
law_summary: If leprosy reappears in a garment or leather item after cleaning, it must be burned with fire
classification_reasoning: Direct command to general Israel for obvious recurring leprosy cases
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:57 - 'thou shalt burn that wherein the plague is' - direct command
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies only after prior priestly examination or can be self-determined

verse_text:
And the garment, either warp, or woof, or whatsoever thing of skin
it be, which thou shalt wash, if the plague be departed from them, then it shall be washed the second time, and shall be clean.
law_summary: If a garment or leather item is washed and the plague departs from it, it must be washed a second time to be considered clean
classification_reasoning: Straightforward purification procedure not requiring priestly oversight for execution
textual_evidence: Leviticus 13:58 - 'it shall be washed the second time, and shall be clean'
interpretive_questions: Whether initial determination of plague departure requires priestly confirmation
notes: Second washing required to confirm cleanliness of garment

verse_text: This shall be the law of the leper in the day of his cleansing: He shall be brought unto the priest:
law_summary: A leper must be brought to the priest on the day of his cleansing
other_torah_refs: Part of broader leprosy laws in Leviticus 13-14
classification_reasoning: Part of ongoing legal statute for leprosy purification, no temporal limits stated
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:2 - 'This shall be the law of the leper' - uses 'shall be' indicating ongoing statute
interpretive_questions: Application depends on identifying biblical leprosy vs modern diseases
notes: Beginning of the leper's purification ritual

verse_text:
And the priest shall go forth out of the camp; and the priest shall look, and, behold,
if the plague of leprosy be healed in the leper;
law_summary: The priest must go outside the camp to examine a leper to determine if the leprosy has been healed
classification_reasoning: Procedural requirement for priests as part of ongoing purification law
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:3 - 'the priest shall go forth' - directive command with no temporal limitation
interpretive_questions: Requires functioning Levitical priesthood and camp structure
notes: Priest must go outside the camp to examine the healed leper

verse_text:
Then shall the priest command to take for him that is to be cleansed two birds alive
and clean, and cedar wood, and scarlet, and hyssop:
law_summary: The priest must command specific items to be taken for the cleansing ritual of a person being purified: two live clean birds, cedar wood, scarlet material, and hyssop
classification_reasoning: Specific procedural command within ongoing purification statute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:4 - 'Then shall the priest command' - ongoing procedural directive
interpretive_questions: Availability of specified ritual materials and priestly authority

verse_text: And the priest shall command that one of the birds be killed in an earthen vessel over running water:
law_summary: The priest must command that one bird be killed in an earthen vessel over running water as part of the purification ritual
classification_reasoning: Continuation of purification ritual procedure with no stated temporal limits
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:5 - 'the priest shall command' - procedural directive within ongoing law
interpretive_questions: Specific requirements for earthen vessel and running water accessibility

verse_text:
As for the living bird, he shall take it, and the cedar wood, and the scarlet, and the hyssop, and shall dip them and the living bird in the blood of the bird
that was killed over the running water:
law_summary: The priest must dip the living bird, cedar wood, scarlet thread, and hyssop in the blood of the slaughtered bird mixed with running water during purification ritual
classification_reasoning: Essential step in purification ritual, part of broader ongoing statute
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:6 - 'he shall take it...and shall dip them' - mandatory procedural step

verse_text: And he shall sprinkle upon him that is to be cleansed from the leprosy seven times, and shall pronounce him clean, and shall let the living bird loose into the open field.
law_summary: The priest must sprinkle the person being cleansed from leprosy seven times, pronounce them clean, and release the living bird into the open field

textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:7 - 'he shall sprinkle...and shall pronounce...and shall let' - three mandatory actions
interpretive_questions: Authority to pronounce clean and appropriate location for bird release

verse_text: And he that is to be cleansed shall wash his clothes, and shave off all his hair, and wash himself in water, that he may be clean: and after that he shall come into the camp, and shall tarry abroad out of his tent seven days.
law_summary: Person being cleansed from leprosy must wash clothes, shave all hair, bathe, then remain outside their tent for seven days after entering the camp
addressee_in_text: he that is to be cleansed
classification_reasoning: Personal purification requirements that don't require temple or priesthood for execution
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:8 - 'he that is to be cleansed shall wash...and shave...and wash' - individual obligations
interpretive_questions: Application to modern camp/community structure

verse_text: But it shall be on the seventh day, that he shall shave all his hair off his head and his beard and his eyebrows, even all his hair he shall shave off: and he shall wash his clothes, also he shall wash his flesh in water, and he shall be clean.
law_summary: On the seventh day of purification, one being cleansed from leprosy must shave off all hair from head, beard, and eyebrows, wash clothes and body in water to become clean
classification_reasoning: Individual purification obligation on specific day, no prerequisites beyond personal action
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:9 - 'he shall shave...he shall wash...he shall wash' - personal obligations
interpretive_questions: Complete removal of all body hair in modern context

verse_text:
And on the eighth day he shall take two he lambs without blemish, and one ewe lamb of the first year without blemish, and three tenth deals of fine flour
for a meat offering, mingled with oil, and one log of oil.
law_summary: On the eighth day of purification from leprosy, the person must bring specific sacrificial offerings: two unblemished male lambs, one unblemished female lamb under one year old, fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, and one log of oil
classification_reasoning: Sacrificial offering requiring functioning temple system and priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:10 - 'he shall take two he lambs...for a meat offering' - sacrificial requirement
interpretive_questions: Alternative provisions for those unable to afford specified animals
notes: Eighth-day offerings for leper purification include guilt, sin, burnt, and grain offerings

verse_text:
And the priest that maketh
him clean shall present the man that is to be made clean, and those things, before the LORD,
at
 the door of the tabernacle of the congregation:

verse_text:

for a wave offering before the LORD:

verse_text:
And he shall slay the lamb in the place where he shall kill the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place: for as the sin offering
is the priest’s,
so is the trespass offering: it
is most holy:

verse_text:
And the priest shall take
some of the blood of the trespass offering, and the priest shall put
it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot:

verse_text:
And the priest shall take
some of the log of oil, and pour
it into the palm of his own left hand:

And the priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that
is
 in his left hand, and shall sprinkle of the oil with his finger seven times before the LORD:

verse_text:
And of the rest of the oil that
is in his hand shall the priest put upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot, upon the blood of the trespass offering:
law_summary: The priest must apply oil to the right ear, thumb, and big toe of the person being cleansed for leprosy, placing it over the blood of the trespass offering

textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:17 - oil placed 'upon the blood' showing layered ritual process

verse_text:
And the remnant of the oil that
is in the priest’s hand he shall pour upon the head of him that is to be cleansed: and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD.

verse_text: And the priest shall offer the sin offering, and make an atonement for him that is to be cleansed from his uncleanness; and afterward he shall kill the burnt offering:

verse_text: And the priest shall offer the burnt offering and the meat offering upon the altar: and the priest shall make an atonement for him, and he shall be clean.

verse_text: And two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, such as he is able to get; and the one shall be a sin offering, and the other a burnt offering.

textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:22 - directive for bringing offerings in leprosy purification process

verse_text: And he shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing unto the priest, unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, before the LORD.

interpretive_questions: Whether timing applies without temple; identification of biblical leprosy versus modern conditions

verse_text:
And he shall kill the lamb of the trespass offering, and the priest shall take
some of the blood of the trespass offering, and put
it upon the tip of the right ear of him that is to be cleansed, and upon the thumb of his right hand, and upon the great toe of his right foot:

verse_text:
And the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger
some
 of the oil that
is in his left hand seven times before the LORD:

textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:27 - priest shall sprinkle oil seven times before the LORD with right finger

verse_text:
And he that owneth the house shall come and tell the priest, saying, It seemeth to me
there is as it were a plague in the house:
law_summary: The homeowner must report suspected plague or contamination in their house to the priest
classification_reasoning: Part of ongoing purity laws within Leviticus statutory framework, but requires functioning priesthood for implementation
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:35 - command structure indicates ongoing obligation when conditions arise
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies only to houses in Israel or any dwelling; whether any qualified religious authority could substitute for priest
notes: Tzaraat of houses; homeowner must report suspected plague to priest

verse_text:
Then the priest shall command that they empty the house, before the priest go
into it to see the plague, that all that
is
 in the house be not made unclean: and afterward the priest shall go in to see the house:
law_summary: The priest must command that a house be emptied before he enters to examine it for plague, to prevent the contents from becoming unclean
classification_reasoning: Procedural requirement within purity law system, lacks temporal limitations but requires functioning priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:36 - procedural command with no time limitations stated
interpretive_questions: Whether this procedure could be adapted without Levitical priesthood; scope of 'house' definition

verse_text:
And he shall look on the plague, and, behold,
if the plague
be in the walls of the house with hollow strakes, greenish or reddish, which in sight
are lower than the wall;
law_summary: The priest must examine plague marks in house walls, looking for greenish or reddish hollow streaks that appear deeper than the wall surface
classification_reasoning: Diagnostic procedure within ongoing purity system, no temporal restrictions given but requires priestly expertise
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:37 - examination criteria presented as standing procedure
interpretive_questions: Whether the specific visual criteria could be applied by non-priests; relationship to modern mold/contamination

verse_text: Then the priest shall go out of the house to the door of the house, and shut up the house seven days:
law_summary: The priest must exit the house and quarantine it for seven days when examining for plague
classification_reasoning: Quarantine procedure with specific seven-day period, part of ongoing purity law framework
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:38 - specific procedural command with definite time frame but no overall temporal limitation
interpretive_questions: Whether seven-day period is absolute or can be modified; applicability without priestly authority

verse_text:
And the priest shall come again the seventh day, and shall look: and, behold,
if the plague be spread in the walls of the house;
law_summary: The priest must return on the seventh day to inspect if the plague has spread in the walls of the house
classification_reasoning: Follow-up inspection procedure within purity law system, no temporal limitations but requires priesthood
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:39 - mandatory return inspection on seventh day
interpretive_questions: Consequences if priest fails to return; whether timing is absolute requirement

verse_text:
Then the priest shall command that they take away the stones in which the plague
is, and they shall cast them into an unclean place without the city:
law_summary: Priest must command removal of plague-infected stones from houses and disposal in unclean place outside the city
classification_reasoning: Remediation procedure requiring priestly authority and disposal outside city, implies settled land context
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:40 - command for stone removal and disposal 'without the city'
interpretive_questions: Definition of 'unclean place'; whether this applies to rural areas without cities

verse_text: And he shall cause the house to be scraped within round about, and they shall pour out the dust that they scrape off without the city into an unclean place:
law_summary: When dealing with a house afflicted with plague, the house must be scraped inside all around and the scraped dust must be poured out in an unclean place outside the city
classification_reasoning: Comprehensive remediation procedure requiring priestly oversight and city context for disposal
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:41 - scraping procedure with disposal 'without the city into an unclean place'
interpretive_questions: Who performs the physical scraping; whether 'he' refers to priest or homeowner

verse_text:
And they shall take other stones, and put
them in the place of those stones; and he shall take other morter, and shall plaister the house.
law_summary: After removing contaminated stones from a house with leprosy, replacement stones must be installed and the house must be replastered with new mortar
classification_reasoning: Restoration procedure that could be followed without direct priestly involvement, general building practice
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:42 - replacement and replastering procedure using 'they shall' indicating workers
interpretive_questions: Whether priestly supervision required; timing relationship to other procedures

verse_text:
Then the priest shall come and look, and, behold,
if the plague be spread in the house, it
is a fretting leprosy in the house: it is unclean.
law_summary: Priest must inspect a house and declare it unclean if the plague has spread, indicating fretting leprosy
classification_reasoning: Diagnostic and declaration procedure requiring priestly authority to declare unclean status
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:44 - priest must 'come and look' and make determination of uncleanness
interpretive_questions: Whether declaration has legal force beyond religious realm; authority to override priestly determination

verse_text:
And he shall break down the house, the stones of it, and the timber thereof, and all the morter of the house; and he shall carry
them
 forth out of the city into an unclean place.
law_summary: A house with persistent leprosy must be completely demolished and its materials carried outside the city to an unclean place
classification_reasoning: Final remediation requiring priestly authority and city context, most severe response in house contamination sequence
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:45 - complete demolition and removal 'out of the city into an unclean place'
interpretive_questions: Whether 'he shall' refers to priest personally or under his authority; compensation for homeowner
notes: Complete demolition required for persistent house tzaraat

verse_text: Moreover he that goeth into the house all the while that it is shut up shall be unclean until the even.
law_summary: Anyone who enters a house that is shut up due to plague shall be unclean until evening
classification_reasoning: Part of ongoing purity laws with no time limitations specified
textual_evidence: Leviticus 14:46 - conditional law based on circumstance of plague house
interpretive_questions: Application when houses with leprous plague conditions exist today

verse_text: And he that lieth in the house shall wash his clothes; and he that eateth in the house shall wash his clothes.
law_summary: Anyone who lies down or eats in a house with leprous plague must wash their clothes
addressee_in_text: anyone who lies or eats in the house
