categories: LOVE_GOD > PURITY_AND_HOLINESS > Holiness of the Camp
notes: Theological basis for camp cleanliness: the LORD walks in the midst of the camp.

verse_text: Thou shalt not deliver unto his master the servant which is escaped from his master unto thee:
law_summary: Do not return an escaped slave to his master
classification_reasoning: Moral imperative with no temporal limitations or special requirements
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:15 - direct prohibition with no conditions
interpretive_questions: Application in modern legal systems where slavery is illegal
notes: Do not return an escaped slave to his master; unique among ancient Near Eastern law codes.

verse_text:
He shall dwell with thee,
even among you, in that place which he shall choose in one of thy gates, where it liketh him best: thou shalt not oppress him.
law_summary: Do not oppress a runaway slave who seeks refuge with you; allow him to dwell where he chooses
classification_reasoning: Moral imperative regarding treatment of refugees; 'thy gates' suggests Israelite territory but principle transcends location
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:16 - 'thou shalt not oppress him' is clear moral command
interpretive_questions: Whether 'in thy gates' limits application to land of Israel
notes: An escaped slave may dwell freely wherever he chooses and must not be oppressed.

verse_text: There shall be no whore of the daughters of Israel, nor a sodomite of the sons of Israel.
law_summary: Prohibition against Israelite women becoming cult prostitutes and Israelite men becoming male cult prostitutes
addressee_in_text: daughters of Israel, sons of Israel
classification_reasoning: Moral prohibition for Israelites with no temporal or conditional limitations
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:17 - absolute prohibition 'There shall be no'
interpretive_questions: Specific focus on cult prostitution vs general prostitution
notes: Prohibition against cult prostitution (both female and male) among Israelites.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not bring the hire of a whore, or the price of a dog, into the house of the LORD thy God for any vow: for even both these
are
 abomination unto the LORD thy God.
law_summary: Do not bring money earned from prostitution or the price of a dog as offerings to the house of the LORD for any vow
classification_reasoning: Requires 'house of the LORD thy God' (temple) for application; principle eternal but prerequisite needed
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:18 - specifically mentions 'house of the LORD thy God'
interpretive_questions: Whether principle applies to synagogue or other worship contexts without temple
notes: Money from prostitution or the 'price of a dog' (possibly male prostitution) may not be brought as offerings to the house of the LORD.

verse_text: But if thou shalt forbear to vow, it shall be no sin in thee.
law_summary: It is not a sin to refrain from making vows
classification_reasoning: Clarification that abstaining from vows is permissible; ongoing principle
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:22 - 'it shall be no sin in thee'
interpretive_questions: Whether this encourages or discourages vow-making
notes: It is not sinful to refrain from making vows; establishes that vows are voluntary.

verse_text:
When thou comest into thy neighbour’s vineyard, then thou mayest eat grapes thy fill at thine own pleasure; but thou shalt not put
any
 in thy vessel.
law_summary: When entering a neighbor's vineyard, you may eat grapes to your satisfaction but cannot take any away in a container
classification_reasoning: This is a property law regulating behavior when entering a neighbor's vineyard. It assumes agricultural land ownership and neighborly relationships within the land of Israel.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:24 - conditional law beginning 'When thou comest into thy neighbour's vineyard'
interpretive_questions: Does this apply in diaspora settings or only in agricultural contexts within the land?
notes: May eat grapes in a neighbor's vineyard but cannot take any in a vessel; limits on gleaning rights.

verse_text: When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour, then thou mayest pluck the ears with thine hand; but thou shalt not move a sickle unto thy neighbour’s standing corn.
law_summary: You may pluck grain by hand from your neighbor's field but may not harvest it with a sickle
classification_reasoning: Similar to the vineyard law, this regulates behavior regarding neighbor's grain fields, assuming agricultural land ownership context.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 23:25 - conditional law beginning 'When thou comest into the standing corn of thy neighbour'
interpretive_questions: Limited to agricultural contexts or applicable to any neighbor's property?
notes: May pluck grain by hand from a neighbor's field but cannot use a sickle; distinguishes casual eating from harvesting.

verse_text:
When a man hath taken a wife, and married her, and it come to pass that she find no favour in his eyes, because he hath found some uncleanness in her: then let him write her a bill of divorcement, and give
it in her hand, and send her out of his house.
law_summary: A man may divorce his wife by writing a bill of divorcement and giving it to her if he finds some uncleanness in her
classification_reasoning: This establishes a procedural requirement for divorce. No temporal limitations or special prerequisites are mentioned in the text.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:1 - procedural law for divorce process
interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'uncleanness' and are there other Torah requirements for divorce?
notes: Procedure for divorce: husband writes a bill of divorcement (get) and gives it to the wife.

verse_text:
And when she is departed out of his house, she may go and be another man’s
wife.
law_summary: A divorced woman may remarry another man
classification_reasoning: This grants permission for divorced women to remarry. It appears to be an ongoing legal principle with no temporal or conditional limitations.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:2 - 'she may go and be another man's wife'
interpretive_questions: Are there other Torah restrictions on remarriage not mentioned in this verse?
notes: A divorced woman is permitted to remarry.

verse_text:
Her former husband, which sent her away, may not take her again to be his wife, after that she is defiled; for that
is abomination before the LORD: and thou shalt not cause the land to sin, which the LORD thy God giveth thee
for an inheritance.
law_summary: A divorced husband cannot remarry his former wife if she has married another man in between
classification_reasoning: This prohibition is called 'abomination before the LORD' and connects to not causing the land to sin, suggesting it's a permanent moral principle.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:4 - 'that is abomination before the LORD' and 'thou shalt not cause the land to sin'
interpretive_questions: Does the land reference limit this to Israel only, or is it a universal moral principle?
notes: A man may not remarry his divorced wife if she has been married to another man in between; prevents treating marriage as a temporary arrangement.

verse_text:
When a man hath taken a new wife, he shall not go out to war, neither shall he be charged with any business:
but he shall be free at home one year, and shall cheer up his wife which he hath taken.
law_summary: A newly married man must remain at home for one year, exempt from military service and other duties, to bring happiness to his new wife
classification_reasoning: This law assumes military service obligations and organized warfare structure, which was specific to Israel's theocratic system in the land.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:5 - 'he shall not go out to war, neither shall he be charged with any business'
interpretive_questions: Does the principle of prioritizing new marriage apply even without formal military service requirements?
notes: Newly married man exempt from military service and business obligations for one year; spans marriage law and warfare exemptions.

verse_text:
No man shall take the nether or the upper millstone to pledge: for he taketh
a man’s life to pledge.
law_summary: Prohibition against taking millstones as collateral for a loan
classification_reasoning: This moral principle protects basic livelihood tools from being taken as collateral, described as taking 'a man's life to pledge' - appears to be a universal justice principle.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:6 - 'for he taketh a man's life to pledge'
interpretive_questions: What modern equivalents would constitute taking someone's 'life' as pledge?
notes: Millstones cannot be taken as security for a loan; they are essential for livelihood.

verse_text: Take heed in the plague of leprosy, that thou observe diligently, and do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you: as I commanded them, so ye shall observe to do.
law_summary: When dealing with leprosy, carefully observe and follow all instructions given by the Levitical priests
classification_reasoning: This explicitly requires 'the priests the Levites' to provide instruction, making it dependent on the functioning Levitical priesthood.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:8 - 'do according to all that the priests the Levites shall teach you'
interpretive_questions: Does this apply to any skin condition or specifically the biblical leprosy described in Leviticus?
notes: Obey the Levitical priests' instructions regarding tzaraat (leprosy); general reference to the detailed laws in Leviticus.

verse_text: When thou dost lend thy brother any thing, thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge.
law_summary: When lending to someone, you must not enter their house to take collateral
classification_reasoning: This protects the dignity and privacy of borrowers by preventing creditors from entering their homes. It's a moral principle with no special prerequisites.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:10 - 'thou shalt not go into his house to fetch his pledge'
interpretive_questions: Does this extend to modern collection practices and what constitutes entering someone's 'house'?
notes: Creditor must not enter the debtor's house to take a pledge; protects the borrower's dignity.

verse_text: Thou shalt stand abroad, and the man to whom thou dost lend shall bring out the pledge abroad unto thee.
law_summary: When lending money, the creditor must wait outside while the debtor brings the pledge out to them
classification_reasoning: Civil procedural law given as ongoing statute without time limitations
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:11 - direct command 'Thou shalt stand abroad'
interpretive_questions: None - straightforward procedural requirement
notes: Creditor must wait outside while the debtor brings out the pledge.

verse_text:
And if the man
be poor, thou shalt not sleep with his pledge:
law_summary: You shall not keep overnight the pledge of a poor person
classification_reasoning: Social justice law repeated in Torah, given as ongoing moral obligation
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:12 prohibition 'thou shalt not sleep with his pledge'
interpretive_questions: None - clear prohibition with moral basis
notes: Must not keep a poor person's pledge overnight; parallels Exodus 22:26-27 regarding returning a cloak before sunset.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not oppress an hired servant
that is poor and needy,
whether he be of thy brethren, or of thy strangers that
are in thy land within thy gates:
law_summary: Do not oppress a hired servant who is poor and needy, whether they are Israelite or foreign
classification_reasoning: Universal social justice law applying to all Israel regarding treatment of workers
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:14 includes both 'thy brethren' and 'strangers'
interpretive_questions: None - broad application clearly stated
notes: Do not oppress a poor hired servant, whether Israelite or foreigner.

verse_text: The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers: every man shall be put to death for his own sin.
law_summary: Individuals shall only be executed for their own sins, not for the sins of their parents or children
classification_reasoning: Fundamental judicial principle establishing individual responsibility
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:16 'every man shall be put to death for his own sin'
interpretive_questions: Application to civil vs. criminal matters, relationship to corporate consequences elsewhere in Torah
notes: Individual responsibility in capital punishment: fathers not executed for children's sins nor children for fathers'.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not pervert the judgment of the stranger,
nor
 of the fatherless; nor take a widow’s raiment to pledge:
law_summary: Do not pervert justice for strangers or orphans, and do not take a widow's garment as security for a loan
classification_reasoning: Judicial and civil law protecting vulnerable populations, repeatedly emphasized in Torah
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:17 specific protection for 'stranger', 'fatherless', and 'widow'
interpretive_questions: None - clear prohibitions for vulnerable population protection
categories: LOVE_NEIGHBOR > COMPASSION_AND_CARE > The Widow and Orphan
notes: Triple protection: do not pervert justice for strangers or orphans, and do not take a widow's garment as pledge.

verse_text: When thou cuttest down thine harvest in thy field, and hast forgot a sheaf in the field, thou shalt not go again to fetch it: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow: that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hands.
law_summary: When harvesting, do not return to collect forgotten sheaves; leave them for strangers, orphans, and widows
classification_reasoning: Agricultural law with divine blessing promised, applicable wherever harvesting occurs
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:19 'that the LORD thy God may bless thee in all the work of thine hands'
interpretive_questions: Application beyond ancient Israel's land - principle vs. specific agricultural context
categories: LOVE_NEIGHBOR > COMPASSION_AND_CARE > The Widow and Orphan
notes: Forgotten sheaves left for strangers, orphans, and widows; part of the gleaning laws.

verse_text: When thou beatest thine olive tree, thou shalt not go over the boughs again: it shall be for the stranger, for the fatherless, and for the widow.
law_summary: When harvesting olives, do not go over the branches a second time; leave the remaining fruit for strangers, orphans, and widows
classification_reasoning: Agricultural law part of gleaning statutes for poor relief, no time limitation stated
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 24:20 consistent with broader gleaning laws throughout Torah
interpretive_questions: Application beyond olive harvesting - principle of leaving provision for needy in all harvest contexts
categories: LOVE_NEIGHBOR > COMPASSION_AND_CARE > The Widow and Orphan
notes: Olive gleaning law: do not beat olive branches twice; remainder for the vulnerable.

verse_text:
If there be a controversy between men, and they come unto judgment, that
the judges may judge them; then they shall justify the righteous, and condemn the wicked.
law_summary: Judges must justify the righteous and condemn the wicked when men come to them for judgment in controversies
classification_reasoning: Fundamental principle of justice with no temporal limitation, applicable wherever judicial authority exists
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:1 - states ongoing procedure 'when they come unto judgment'
interpretive_questions: Definition of legitimate judicial authority; application in non-Israelite legal systems
notes: Judges must justify the righteous and condemn the wicked; foundational principle of justice.

verse_text:
And it shall be, if the wicked man
be worthy to be beaten, that the judge shall cause him to lie down, and to be beaten before his face, according to his fault, by a certain number.
law_summary: A judge must supervise the beating of a convicted person, with the punishment administered in the judge's presence according to the severity of the crime
classification_reasoning: Procedural requirement for judicial punishment with no temporal restriction, part of ongoing justice system
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:2 - 'it shall be, if' indicates ongoing conditional procedure
interpretive_questions: Whether corporal punishment remains valid; adaptation to modern judicial systems
notes: Judicial corporal punishment must be administered in the judge's presence and proportional to the offense.

verse_text:
Forty stripes he may give him,
and not exceed: lest,
if he should exceed, and beat him above these with many stripes, then thy brother should seem vile unto thee.
law_summary: Judicial punishment must not exceed forty lashes to preserve the dignity of the offender
classification_reasoning: Limit on judicial punishment protecting human dignity, with no temporal restriction and universal moral principle
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:3 - absolute prohibition 'and not exceed' with reasoning about human dignity
interpretive_questions: Application when corporal punishment is not used; principle of proportionate punishment
notes: Maximum of forty lashes to preserve human dignity; tradition reduced to 39 as a safeguard.

verse_text:
Thou shalt not muzzle the ox when he treadeth out
the corn.
law_summary: Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out grain
classification_reasoning: Animal welfare principle with no temporal limitation, applicable wherever the situation exists
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:4 - direct prohibition with no conditions or time limits
interpretive_questions: Application to modern farming methods; extension to other animals and work situations
notes: Do not muzzle an ox while it treads grain; compassion to working animals, also applied by Paul to human laborers.

verse_text: If brethren dwell together, and one of them die, and have no child, the wife of the dead shall not marry without unto a stranger: her husband’s brother shall go in unto her, and take her to him to wife, and perform the duty of an husband’s brother unto her.
law_summary: When brothers live together and one dies childless, his widow must not marry outside the family but must marry her brother-in-law who shall perform levirate marriage duties
classification_reasoning: Family obligation law with no temporal restriction, designed to preserve lineage in Israel
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:5 - conditional 'if' statement indicating ongoing procedure
interpretive_questions: Application outside Israel; definition of 'dwelling together'; modern marriage law conflicts
notes: Levirate marriage (yibum): brother-in-law must marry the childless widow.

verse_text:
And it shall be,
that the firstborn which she beareth shall succeed in the name of his brother
which is dead, that his name be not put out of Israel.
law_summary: The firstborn son from a levirate marriage must carry on the name of the deceased brother to preserve his lineage in Israel
classification_reasoning: Consequence of levirate marriage to preserve names 'in Israel', ongoing obligation with genealogical purpose
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:6 - 'that his name be not put out of Israel' indicates ongoing concern for preservation
interpretive_questions: Meaning of 'succeed in the name'; application when not in land of Israel
notes: Firstborn of levirate marriage carries on the deceased brother's name.

verse_text: And if the man like not to take his brother’s wife, then let his brother’s wife go up to the gate unto the elders, and say, My husband’s brother refuseth to raise up unto his brother a name in Israel, he will not perform the duty of my husband’s brother.
law_summary: If a man refuses to marry his deceased brother's wife, she may bring the case to the elders at the gate
classification_reasoning: Procedural law requiring city gate structure and elder system, ongoing principle but infrastructure dependent
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:7 - procedural 'let her go up to the gate' indicates ongoing available remedy
interpretive_questions: Modern equivalent of 'gate unto the elders'; jurisdictional authority outside Israel
notes: Procedure for refusal: widow brings the case to the elders at the gate.

law_summary: City elders must call and speak to a man who refuses levirate marriage to give him opportunity to explain his refusal
classification_reasoning: Procedural obligation for elders in levirate marriage disputes, requires established elder authority structure
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:8 - 'shall call him and speak unto him' indicates ongoing procedural duty
interpretive_questions: Authority of elders outside traditional city structure; due process requirements
notes: Elders must summon and speak to the refusing brother-in-law.

verse_text: Then shall his brother’s wife come unto him in the presence of the elders, and loose his shoe from off his foot, and spit in his face, and shall answer and say, So shall it be done unto that man that will not build up his brother’s house.
law_summary: A brother's widow must publicly humiliate a man who refuses to perform levirate marriage by removing his shoe and spitting in his face before the elders
other_torah_refs: Part of levirate marriage laws in Deuteronomy 25:5-10
classification_reasoning: Ongoing statute with no explicit time limitation, requires organized community with elders
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:9 - procedural law within broader levirate marriage statutes
interpretive_questions: Does this require formal tribal elder structure or any community judicial authority?
notes: Chalitzah ceremony: widow removes the refusing brother-in-law's shoe and spits in his face before the elders.

verse_text:
Then thou shalt cut off her hand, thine eye shall not pity
her.
law_summary: A woman who seizes a man's genitals during a fight must have her hand cut off without mercy
requires_specific_role: judges with authority to execute punishment
classification_reasoning: Criminal statute with no time limitation, requires judicial authority to implement physical punishment
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:12 - direct judicial command with no temporal restrictions
interpretive_questions: What constitutes legitimate judicial authority for implementing physical punishments?
notes: Punitive amputation for a woman who seizes a man's genitals during a fight; unique penalty in Torah law.

verse_text: Remember what Amalek did unto thee by the way, when ye were come forth out of Egypt;
law_summary: Remember what Amalek did to you on the journey from Egypt
classification_reasoning: Command to remember historical event with no temporal limitation
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:17 - direct command to remember with no end date specified
interpretive_questions: Is this merely historical remembrance or does it carry ongoing practical implications?
notes: Command to remember what Amalek did; a perpetual obligation of national memory.

verse_text:
Therefore it shall be, when the LORD thy God hath given thee rest from all thine enemies round about, in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee
for an inheritance to possess it,
that thou shalt blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; thou shalt not forget
it.
law_summary: Israel must blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven when God gives them rest from enemies in the promised land
classification_reasoning: Explicitly conditional on being given rest from enemies in the promised land
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 25:19 - 'when the LORD thy God hath given thee rest from all thine enemies round about, in the land'
interpretive_questions: What constitutes 'rest from all enemies' and does this require sovereign control of the land?
notes: Blot out the remembrance of Amalek from under heaven; must not forget this obligation.

verse_text: And thou shalt go unto the priest that shall be in those days, and say unto him, I profess this day unto the LORD thy God, that I am come unto the country which the LORD sware unto our fathers for to give us.
law_summary: When bringing firstfruits, you must go to the priest and declare that you have come into the land the LORD swore to give your fathers
classification_reasoning: Requires priest, land possession, and organized worship at designated place
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:3 - 'unto the priest that shall be in those days' and reference to 'the country which the LORD sware'
interpretive_questions: What constitutes a legitimate priest for this ceremony and does this require formal temple worship?
notes: Verbal declaration when bringing firstfruits to the priest; part of the bikkurim (firstfruits) ceremony. Categorized under Tithes and Terumah as firstfruits are a form of terumah offering.

verse_text: And the priest shall take the basket out of thine hand, and set it down before the altar of the LORD thy God.
law_summary: The priest must take the basket of firstfruits from the worshipper and set it before the altar
classification_reasoning: Requires altar, functioning priesthood, and formal worship structure
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:4 - 'set it down before the altar of the LORD thy God'
interpretive_questions: Does this require the specific temple altar or could any legitimate altar suffice?
notes: Priest's role in the firstfruits ceremony: receive the basket and place it before the altar.

verse_text:
And thou shalt speak and say before the LORD thy God, A Syrian ready to perish
was my father, and he went down into Egypt, and sojourned there with a few, and became there a nation, great, mighty, and populous:
law_summary: Recite a specific declaration about ancestral history when presenting firstfruits to the LORD
classification_reasoning: Part of firstfruits ceremony requiring presence in the land with agricultural produce
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:5 - specific declaration tied to firstfruits offering
interpretive_questions: Whether declaration could be adapted for diaspora contexts or requires literal land connection
notes: Prescribed historical recitation during the firstfruits ceremony; recounts the narrative from the patriarchs through the Exodus. This is a liturgical declaration, hence categorized under Prayer and Confession.

verse_text: And now, behold, I have brought the firstfruits of the land, which thou, O LORD, hast given me. And thou shalt set it before the LORD thy God, and worship before the LORD thy God:
law_summary: Present firstfruits before the LORD and worship
classification_reasoning: Firstfruits offering is ongoing obligation but requires agricultural land and place of worship
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:10 - command to present and worship before the LORD
interpretive_questions: Whether 'before the LORD' requires temple or can be fulfilled at any sacred site
notes: Present firstfruits before the LORD and worship; culmination of the bikkurim ceremony.

verse_text:
And thou shalt rejoice in every good
thing which the LORD thy God hath given unto thee, and unto thine house, thou, and the Levite, and the stranger that
is among you.
law_summary: You shall rejoice in every good thing the LORD has given you, your household, the Levite, and the stranger among you
classification_reasoning: General command to rejoice in God's blessings, not tied to specific location or institution
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:11 - command to rejoice in every good thing from the LORD
interpretive_questions: Whether this is specifically tied to firstfruits context or general principle
notes: Rejoice in God's blessings together with household, Levite, and stranger at the firstfruits offering.

verse_text:
Then thou shalt say before the LORD thy God, I have brought away the hallowed things out of
mine house, and also have given them unto the Levite, and unto the stranger, to the fatherless, and to the widow, according to all thy commandments which thou hast commanded me: I have not transgressed thy commandments, neither have I forgotten
them:
law_summary: Declaration to be made before the LORD affirming proper distribution of tithes to Levites, strangers, orphans, and widows
classification_reasoning: Declaration tied to tithe distribution system requiring agricultural economy and sacred space
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 26:13 - declaration 'before the LORD thy God' about tithe distribution
interpretive_questions: Whether declaration requires formal sanctuary or any place of worship
notes: Vidui maaser: confession/declaration affirming proper distribution of tithes; a prescribed liturgical statement made before the LORD.

verse_text: And it shall be on the day when ye shall pass over Jordan unto the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee, that thou shalt set thee up great stones, and plaister them with plaister:
law_summary: Set up great stones and plaster them on the day of crossing the Jordan into the Promised Land
classification_reasoning: One-time historical command for specific event of crossing Jordan - 'on the day when ye shall pass over'
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:2 - 'on the day when ye shall pass over Jordan'
interpretive_questions: Whether this was fulfilled historically or awaits future fulfillment
notes: Though fulfilled historically at the Jordan crossing, this establishes a covenantal monument. Erring on the side of inclusion as it is a command to all Israel establishing a permanent memorial.

verse_text: And thou shalt write upon them all the words of this law, when thou art passed over, that thou mayest go in unto the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee, a land that floweth with milk and honey; as the LORD God of thy fathers hath promised thee.
law_summary: Write all the words of the law on stones after crossing into the Promised Land
classification_reasoning: One-time command tied to specific historical moment of entering the land
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:3 - 'when thou art passed over' into the promised land
interpretive_questions: Whether this was fulfilled by Joshua or awaits future restoration
notes: Writing the words of the Torah on stones; preserves the law for public remembrance.

verse_text: And thou shalt offer peace offerings, and shalt eat there, and rejoice before the LORD thy God.
law_summary: Offer peace offerings, eat there, and rejoice before the LORD your God
classification_reasoning: Specific instruction for ceremony at Ebal, though peace offerings are ongoing commandments elsewhere
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:7 - 'And thou shalt offer peace offerings' - part of specific ceremonial context
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies only to the Ebal ceremony or establishes ongoing practice at that location
notes: Peace offerings to be offered, eaten, and rejoiced in before the LORD at the covenant ceremony. Though tied to a specific event, peace offerings are an ongoing institution.

verse_text: These shall stand upon mount Gerizim to bless the people, when ye are come over Jordan; Simeon, and Levi, and Judah, and Issachar, and Joseph, and Benjamin:
law_summary: Six tribes (Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, Benjamin) shall stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people after crossing the Jordan
classification_reasoning: One-time ceremonial positioning of tribes for covenant ceremony upon entering the land
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:12 - 'when ye are come over Jordan' - specific timing and event
interpretive_questions: Whether this ceremony should be repeated or was a one-time historical event
notes: Six tribes on Mount Gerizim for the blessing; part of the covenant ceremony. Included since it is a commanded national ceremony, though with a one-time historical fulfillment.

verse_text: And these shall stand upon mount Ebal to curse; Reuben, Gad, and Asher, and Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.
law_summary: Six tribes (Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali) must stand on Mount Ebal to pronounce curses
classification_reasoning: One-time ceremonial positioning of tribes for covenant ceremony upon entering the land
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:13 - parallel structure with verse 12, same ceremonial context
interpretive_questions: Whether this ceremony should be repeated or was a one-time historical event
notes: Six tribes on Mount Ebal for the curse; counterpart to v. 12.

verse_text: And the Levites shall speak, and say unto all the men of Israel with a loud voice,
law_summary: The Levites must speak with a loud voice to all the men of Israel
classification_reasoning: Specific role for Levites in the Mount Ebal/Gerizim ceremony
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:14 - 'And the Levites shall speak' - part of the specific ceremony described
interpretive_questions: Whether Levites have ongoing role in similar ceremonial proclamations
notes: Levites must speak the curses with a loud voice to all Israel; part of the Gerizim/Ebal ceremony. Levitical function in covenant proclamation.

verse_text:
Cursed
be he that maketh the blind to wander out of the way. And all the people shall say, Amen.
law_summary: Prohibition against leading the blind astray or misdirecting them
classification_reasoning: Universal moral principle about not taking advantage of the vulnerable. Repeated elsewhere in Torah as ongoing moral obligation.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:18 curse formula; Leviticus 19:14 'you shall not put a stumbling block before the blind'
interpretive_questions: Does 'leading astray' include metaphorical blindness or only literal?
notes: Curse on one who misleads the blind; parallels Leviticus 19:14 ('do not put a stumbling block before the blind').

verse_text:
Cursed
be he that lieth with his mother in law. And all the people shall say, Amen.
law_summary: Prohibition against sexual relations with one's mother-in-law
classification_reasoning: Sexual morality law regarding in-laws, repeated in Torah with death penalty. Part of fundamental family relationship boundaries.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:23 curse; Leviticus 20:14 'if a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity'
interpretive_questions: Does this apply during marriage or only after spouse's death?
notes: Prohibition against sexual relations with mother-in-law; part of the incest prohibitions.

verse_text:
Cursed
be he that smiteth his neighbour secretly. And all the people shall say, Amen.
law_summary: Prohibition against secretly striking or killing one's neighbor
classification_reasoning: Prohibition against secret violence/murder. Extension of 'you shall not murder' commandment with emphasis on secretive nature making it worse.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:24 curse formula; relates to sixth commandment against murder
interpretive_questions: Does 'secretly' refer to the act itself or the planning, and does this include non-fatal striking?
notes: Curse on one who secretly strikes a neighbor; prohibition against covert violence.

verse_text:
Cursed
be he that confirmeth not
all the words of this law to do them. And all the people shall say, Amen.
law_summary: All people must confirm and obey all the words of the law
classification_reasoning: Meta-commandment requiring obedience to ALL Torah laws. 'All the words of this law' indicates comprehensive ongoing obligation to entire Torah.
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 27:26 'all the words of this law'; Deuteronomy 28:58 'all the words of this law that are written in this book'
interpretive_questions: Does 'confirm' mean verbal affirmation only, or does it require active obedience? What constitutes 'all the words'?
notes: Comprehensive curse on anyone who does not uphold and obey all the words of the Torah; summary curse of the entire covenant.

verse_text: Keep therefore the words of this covenant, and do them, that ye may prosper in all that ye do.
law_summary: Keep and observe the words of the covenant to prosper
classification_reasoning: Covenant obligation to keep God's words with promised blessing; no time limitation expressed
textual_evidence: Uses imperative 'Keep therefore' without temporal restrictions
interpretive_questions: Prosperity promise may have specific land/national context
categories: LOVE_GOD > KNOW_FEAR_CLING > Love and Fear of God
notes: General command to keep and observe the covenant in order to prosper.

verse_text: That thou shouldest enter into covenant with the LORD thy God, and into his oath, which the LORD thy God maketh with thee this day:
law_summary: Israel must enter into covenant with the LORD and accept His oath
classification_reasoning: Specific historical command for Israel to enter the Mosaic covenant at that time and place
textual_evidence: 'this day' indicates specific temporal context of covenant ratification ceremony
interpretive_questions: Whether covenant obligations continue even though entry ceremony was historical event
notes: Israel must enter into the covenant and oath with the LORD; foundational covenant obligation.

verse_text: And shalt return unto the LORD thy God, and shalt obey his voice according to all that I command thee this day, thou and thy children, with all thine heart, and with all thy soul;
law_summary: Return to the LORD your God and obey His voice with all your heart and soul
classification_reasoning: Command to return/repent with intergenerational scope; repentance is ongoing obligation
textual_evidence: Includes 'thou and thy children' indicating generational continuity
interpretive_questions: Context suggests post-exile return, but principle of repentance remains constant
categories: LOVE_GOD > KNOW_FEAR_CLING > Love and Fear of God
notes: Command to return (teshuvah/repentance) to the LORD and obey with all heart and soul; foundational text for the concept of repentance.

verse_text:
That thou mayest love the LORD thy God,
and that thou mayest obey his voice, and that thou mayest cleave unto him: for he
is
 thy life, and the length of thy days: that thou mayest dwell in the land which the LORD sware unto thy fathers, to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, to give them.
law_summary: Love the LORD your God, obey his voice, and cleave to him
classification_reasoning: Fundamental obligations to love God, obey His voice, cleave to Him; land dwelling conditional but core duties eternal
textual_evidence: 'he is thy life' indicates permanent, essential relationship regardless of geography
interpretive_questions: Land promise connects to patriarchal covenant but spiritual obligations remain constant
categories: LOVE_GOD > KNOW_FEAR_CLING > Love and Fear of God
notes: Love God, obey His voice, and cleave (davak) to Him; triple command encompassing love, obedience, and clinging to God.

verse_text:
And Moses commanded them, saying, At the end of
every seven years, in the solemnity of the year of release, in the feast of tabernacles,
law_summary: Every seven years during the year of release at the feast of tabernacles, a specific ceremony or reading must be performed
classification_reasoning: Part of ongoing septennial cycle tied to land-based observance and central gathering place
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:10 - cyclical time reference 'every seven years'
interpretive_questions: Whether this requires the temple specifically or any chosen gathering place
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Torah Reading at Sukkot
notes: Hakhel ceremony: at the end of every seven years during Sukkot in the sabbatical year.

verse_text: When all Israel is come to appear before the LORD thy God in the place which he shall choose, thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing.
law_summary: The law must be read aloud before all Israel when they gather at the chosen place before the LORD
classification_reasoning: Ongoing obligation tied to gathering 'in the place which he shall choose' - requires central location
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:11 - 'in the place which he shall choose'
interpretive_questions: Whether any designated gathering place suffices or specifically the temple location
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Torah Reading at Sukkot
notes: The Torah must be read aloud before all Israel at the chosen place during the Hakhel assembly.

verse_text:
Gather the people together, men, and women, and children, and thy stranger that
is within thy gates, that they may hear, and that they may learn, and fear the LORD your God, and observe to do all the words of this law:
law_summary: Gather all people including men, women, children, and foreigners to hear and learn God's law
classification_reasoning: Ongoing inclusivity requirement for law-reading gatherings, tied to central location
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:12 - 'Gather the people together' including 'thy stranger that is within thy gates'
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies to any Torah reading or specifically the septennial ceremony
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Torah Reading at Sukkot
notes: Gather men, women, children, and foreigners for the public Torah reading; inclusive assembly for learning and fearing God.

verse_text:
And
that their children, which have not known
any thing,
 may hear, and learn to fear the LORD your God, as long as ye live in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it.
law_summary: Children who have not known the law must hear it and learn to fear the LORD
classification_reasoning: Explicitly conditioned on living 'in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it'
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:13 - 'as long as ye live in the land whither ye go over Jordan to possess it'
interpretive_questions: Whether this applies only to the initial generation or all subsequent generations in the land
categories: LOVE_GOD > SACRED_TIMES > Sabbatical Year > Torah Reading at Sukkot
notes: Purpose of Hakhel: even children who have not known the Torah must hear and learn to fear the LORD.

verse_text: Now therefore write ye this song for you, and teach it the children of Israel: put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for me against the children of Israel.
law_summary: Write down this song and teach it to the children of Israel so it may serve as a witness
generational_phrase: teach it the children of Israel
classification_reasoning: Command to preserve and teach the song to children of Israel as perpetual witness
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:19 - 'teach it the children of Israel' for ongoing witness function
interpretive_questions: Whether this refers to a specific song in context or Torah teaching generally
notes: Traditionally understood as the command to write a Torah scroll (the 'song' being the Torah or the Song of Moses). Some interpret this as specifically about the Song of Ha'azinu. Either way, it is a perpetual teaching obligation.

verse_text: Take this book of the law, and put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God, that it may be there for a witness against thee.
law_summary: Place the book of the law beside the ark of the covenant as a witness
classification_reasoning: Permanent placement command for the law book as perpetual witness, but requires ark and sanctuary
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 31:26 - 'that it may be there for a witness' - implies permanence
interpretive_questions: Whether the witness function continues without the physical ark and temple
notes: Place the book of the Torah beside the Ark of the Covenant as a perpetual witness. Though initially spoken to the Levites by Moses, this establishes a permanent institutional practice for the Levites.

verse_text: Because I will publish the name of the LORD: ascribe ye greatness unto our God.
law_summary: Ascribe greatness to God
other_torah_refs: Deuteronomy 6:4-5, Exodus 15:2, multiple praise commands throughout Torah
classification_reasoning: Command to ascribe greatness to God is a fundamental worship obligation with no temporal limitations, contextual restrictions, or prerequisites mentioned
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 32:3 - imperative form 'ascribe ye greatness' with no qualifying conditions
interpretive_questions: Whether this is specifically about public proclamation or includes private worship
notes: Exhortation to ascribe greatness to God; within the Song of Moses but expresses a perpetual obligation to glorify God's name.

verse_text: Remember the days of old, consider the years of many generations: ask thy father, and he will shew thee; thy elders, and they will tell thee.
law_summary: Remember ancient history and seek instruction from fathers and elders about past generations
generational_phrase: consider the years of many generations
classification_reasoning: Command to remember history and seek wisdom from elders is ongoing moral obligation with generational language, no prerequisites or temporal limitations
textual_evidence: Deuteronomy 32:7 - imperatives 'Remember...consider...ask' with reference to 'many generations'
interpretive_questions: Scope of 'ancient history' and whether this applies beyond immediate family elders
notes: Remember the days of old and seek instruction from fathers and elders; perpetual obligation to preserve and transmit historical and covenantal memory across generations.
